entries.prompt: Walk me through a moment recently when you said something out loud and immediately heard it land wrong — either in someone else's face, or in your own ear. What did you say, and what did you wish you'd said instead?
entries.content:
It was Tuesday, in the team meeting. Drew was going around asking people for status on the thing we've all been chipping at for two weeks. Reese went before me and gave this clean little summary, three sentences, and Drew did the half-nod he does when he's actually satisfied. Then it was my turn and I had been rehearsing my version in my head the whole time Reese was talking, which means I wasn't really listening to Reese, which means by the time Drew said my name my mouth opened before my brain caught up and what came out was, "Yeah, so, kind of similar to what Reese said, basically."

Kind of similar to what Reese said. Basically.

I heard it in my own ear before it even finished landing. Reese's update was about a completely different piece of the work. Not similar. Not even adjacent. I was just so eager to get to the part where I sounded efficient that I tried to staple myself onto someone else's report and instead I just announced, in front of seven people including Drew, that I didn't have anything of my own to say. Then I tried to recover and the recovery was worse — I started actually explaining what I'd done, but now it had this defensive shape, like I was proving I'd done it, and I could feel my voice doing that thing where it goes up at the end of every sentence. Drew said "okay, thanks" in the voice that isn't mean but isn't anything else either, and we moved on.

The rest of the meeting I sat there replaying it. Kind of similar to what Reese said. Why did I say that. Why did I say it like that. Reese probably noticed. Reese definitely noticed. Drew definitely noticed. There is a slack channel in my head and the only thing in it is that sentence, in bold, with seventeen reactions all of them the grimace face.

What I wish I'd said is just — the actual thing. "I finished the second pass on the spec doc and I'm waiting on feedback from the design side before I move forward." That's it. That's literally all that was needed. Twelve seconds. I had the words. I had them written down in front of me on my notebook. I was looking at them. And instead I tried to do something fancy where I bookended with a casual reference to the previous person and now I'm a person who got tangled in their own attempt to be smooth.

The thing that's bugging me, the thing I keep coming back to, is that I wasn't even nervous before the meeting. I was fine. I had stuff to say. The nervous only kicked in when Reese went and did a good job, and suddenly I was performing against Reese instead of just talking. That's the part I don't like about myself. That I can be fine and then someone else is good and now I'm scrambling. Like there's a fixed amount of competence in any given room and Reese took some of mine.

I told Avery about it last night in the kitchen, sort of. I said "I had a weird moment in a meeting today" and Avery said "weird how" and I said "I don't know, just dumb, I said something dumb" and Avery waited for more and I didn't give more because saying it out loud felt like it would make it bigger. Avery said "I'm sure it wasn't as bad as you think" which is the right thing to say and also the thing I cannot believe when anyone says it. I wanted to say more. I wanted to say something like, *I think Drew thinks I'm not very good*, but I knew if I said that Avery would have a whole reasonable response and I'd have to argue with the reasonable response and I was tired. So I just made a face and changed the subject and we ate the leftover noodles.

Later in bed I thought about it again. Kind of similar to what Reese said, basically. The word "basically" is what kills me. "Basically" is what you say when you're trying to sound like you have a handle on something. It's a tell. Drew uses "basically" only when he actually means it. I use it as a structural beam holding up a sentence that has no other support.

Okay. Trying to be fair to myself for a second. Nobody else in that meeting is sitting at home tonight thinking about what I said. Reese isn't. Drew probably isn't. The other people barely registered it. The version of this that's running in my head — the one where everyone exchanged a look — didn't happen. I know that. I know that the way you know a thing intellectually and don't believe it at all in your body.

I think what I actually wish I'd said isn't even about the meeting. I wish, when Avery asked weird how, I'd said the real thing. "I tried to sound like Reese and I sounded like nobody. I am scared Drew is going to figure out I'm scrambling. I wanted you to tell me I'm fine and then I was annoyed at myself for wanting that." That would have been the actual sentence. I had it. I was looking at it on my own internal notebook. And I said "just dumb, I said something dumb" instead.

Two for two today. Cool. Going to drink some water and not order food and probably order food.

entries.prompt: Think about something you've been quietly competing with someone over — a colleague, a friend, anyone. Describe a recent moment where you felt that comparison happen, what you noticed about them, and what was running underneath in you.
entries.content:
Friday afternoon, like 3:40, that dead stretch where everyone's pretending to still work. Reese posted in the team channel — not even posted, just dropped a link with one sentence under it. "Pulled together a quick doc on the handoff process, lmk if useful." Quick doc. I clicked it. It was not a quick doc. It was like nine pages, with a diagram, with the kind of headers that have actual parallel structure. The little "edited 2 min ago" timestamp under the title. Reese had been sitting on this thing, polishing, and then dropped it like it was a receipt.

Drew reacted to the message with the rocket emoji within ninety seconds. Ninety seconds. I know because I watched it happen. I was sitting there with my own tab open on something I'd been pushing on all week and I just — clicked away from it. Couldn't look at it for a minute. Read the whole nine pages of Reese's doc instead, slowly, looking for something wrong with it. Not consciously. I was telling myself I was reading it to learn from it. But what I was actually doing was scanning for a typo, an awkward sentence, a section that was thinner than the others. There was one paragraph in the middle that I thought was a little hand-wavy and I felt a small sick relief about it and then I felt sick about the relief.

The thing I noticed about Reese — and this is the part I don't want to write but I'm writing it — is that Reese didn't ask anyone before doing this. Reese just decided the team needed a handoff doc and made one. Nobody assigned it. Drew didn't ask for it. Reese saw a gap and filled it and got a rocket emoji within ninety seconds and now there is a nine-page doc in our team channel with Reese's name on it forever.

I have never once in eight months done that. I do the things on my list. I do them well, I think, mostly. But the list is given to me. I have never once just looked up and gone, oh, that's missing, I'll make it. Or maybe I have looked up and seen it and then thought, well, nobody asked, and I don't want to step on toes, and what if it's bad, and what if Drew thinks I should be doing my actual work instead, and so the thought dies and I go back to my list.

Reese doesn't have that voice. Or Reese has it and ignores it. Either way the result is the same: nine pages, rocket emoji, my afternoon ruined.

Underneath, when I'm being honest. It's not that I want to be Reese. I don't even like Reese that much, which is its own embarrassing thing to admit because Reese has done nothing wrong, Reese is perfectly nice, Reese said good morning to me on Tuesday and remembered I don't drink coffee after noon. The not-liking is just the shape my own envy makes when it has nowhere else to go. I don't like Reese because liking Reese would mean accepting that Reese is just better at this than I am, and I would rather assign a small petty reason — Reese is a try-hard, Reese is performative, Reese is doing it for Drew — than sit with the simpler thing, which is that Reese is six months ahead of me and also probably just constitutionally more comfortable taking up space.

What was running underneath. Okay. Underneath was: Drew is going to start measuring me against this. Underneath was: in the next 1:1 Drew is going to ask me what I'm working on and I'm going to list my list and Drew is going to nod and the nod is going to mean, well, you do your list, and Reese builds the team. Underneath was a really specific image of the end-of-year review and Drew using the word "initiative" and me not having any examples. Underneath was, why didn't I think of making a handoff doc. Was it not obvious? It feels obvious now. It feels like I should have seen it months ago. It feels like Reese saw it on day one and just waited for the right moment.

And then underneath that, smaller, quieter — I was tired. I'd been pushing on my thing all week and I was proud of it like an hour ago and now I couldn't even look at it because Reese's nine pages had taken up all the air.

I closed my laptop at 5:02 which is early for me and walked to the bus and on the bus I tried to be fair. Reese is not my enemy. Reese is a person who made a doc. The doc is good. The team is better for it. The fact that I'm sitting on a bus rotating it in my head like a piece of evidence in a trial about my own worth is a me thing, not a Reese thing. I know this. I know this the way I knew the meeting thing on Tuesday. Intellectually, fully. In the body, zero percent.

When I got home Avery was making rice and asked how my day was and I said "fine, normal" and Avery said "you sure?" and I said "yeah, just tired" and that was true and also not the whole sentence. The whole sentence was, someone at work did a thing today and I have spent four hours measuring myself against it and losing. But I'm not going to say that out loud because saying it makes me sound like the kind of person who measures themselves against coworkers, which I am, but I don't want Avery to know I am, even though Avery probably already knows. Avery knows.

We ate the rice. It was good. I didn't open my laptop again. I'm going to try not to open Reese's doc again tonight. I'm probably going to open Reese's doc again tonight.

entries.prompt: There's a version of you that you perform at work — the efficient one, the one with the clean update. When did you most recently catch yourself reaching for that performance, and what was the actual thing underneath it that you weren't saying?
entries.content:
Wednesday, the 1:1 with Drew. The little fifteen-minute one we do mid-week, which is supposed to be casual but isn't, because nothing with Drew is casual when Drew is the one person whose read of you is rewriting itself in real time.

Drew said, "How's it going, what are you working on?" and I had been preparing for this question for approximately forty minutes. I had a mental bullet list. I had transitions between the bullets. I had, no kidding, a closing line. The closing line was, "So I think I'm in good shape to wrap that by Friday and pick up the next chunk Monday." I'd practiced it. On the walk over from my desk to the little glass room, I'd practiced it. Good shape to wrap. Pick up the next chunk. Casual but containing.

And I delivered it. I delivered the whole thing. Three items, clean transitions, the closing line landed right where I wanted it to land. Drew nodded — not the half-nod, a real nod — and said, "Cool, sounds good." And then Drew said, "Anything else on your mind?"

Anything else on your mind.

I said, "No, I think that's everything." Smiling. Already standing up a little, performatively, like a person whose time is also valuable.

Drew said okay, we wrapped, I walked back to my desk, and the second I sat down I felt this drop. Like the floor going down two inches in an elevator. Because the actual thing on my mind was a five-paragraph essay and I had compressed it into the word "no."

What was actually on my mind. Okay. The actual thing was: I don't know if what I'm working on is the thing I should be working on. I've been pushing on this spec for two weeks and I think it's good but I also have no idea if it's the kind of work that registers, or if it's just the list, the list Drew gave me and that I am dutifully completing while Reese builds nine-page docs nobody asked for. The actual thing was: I want you to tell me if I'm doing okay. Not in a review. Not in a structured way. Just — am I okay. Am I tracking. The actual thing was: I noticed you reacted to Reese's doc with a rocket emoji and I noticed you have never reacted to anything I've ever posted with a rocket emoji and I am aware that this is an insane data point to be carrying around but I am carrying it. The actual thing was: I think I might be coasting and I don't know how to tell if I am, and asking you would be admitting that I might be, and I would rather sit here pretending to be in good shape to wrap by Friday.

I had none of those sentences ready. I had the closing line ready. The closing line was the whole performance. The closing line is the thing I rehearse so I don't have to feel any of the rest.

Here's the part that's bugging me. Drew asked. Drew literally opened the door. "Anything else on your mind." That's not a perfunctory question, or maybe it is, but it's at least an opening, and I closed it. I closed it fast. I closed it because if I'd said any of the real thing I would have had to sit there inside the discomfort of having said it, and Drew would have had to respond, and the response might have been kind or might have been the okay-thanks voice from Tuesday's meeting, and I cannot, this week, this month, take another okay-thanks voice from Drew. So I said no. I picked the small bad feeling now over the maybe-bigger bad feeling later.

The performance version is so smooth. The efficient one. The one with the clean update. Three items, transitions, closing line. I can do that one in my sleep. The performance version is not the real me but it's also not nothing — it's a version of me that I built carefully because I noticed early that Drew likes things tidy. So I give Drew tidy. And Drew nods. And the nod is the rocket emoji's smaller, more polite cousin, and I take it home with me and turn it over in my hand for the rest of the day.

But the performance version doesn't ask for anything. That's the thing I'm catching now, sitting at the table at — checking — 11:14. The performance version is designed to not need anything from Drew. It's designed to walk in, deliver, and walk out. Which means every time I do it, I am training Drew to think I don't need anything. And then I'm sitting at home wondering why Drew doesn't seem to notice that I do.

I don't know what I would have said if I'd been brave. Probably not the five paragraphs. Probably something smaller, like, "I've been wondering if I should be looking for things outside my list, and I'm not sure what the appetite is for that." That sentence would have been fine. That sentence would have actually been useful. Drew might have said, oh yeah, here's what I'd love to see, or, no, just keep doing what you're doing, you're doing fine. Either answer would have given me information. Instead I have no information and the smooth closing line and the drop in my stomach.

Avery asked at dinner how the 1:1 went and I said "good, fine" and Avery said "you said that fast" and I laughed because Avery is right, I did say it fast, I said it the way you say it when you don't want a follow-up. And then I did give a little more. I said, "Drew asked if anything was on my mind and I said no and now I'm thinking about all the things I could've said." Avery said, "Could you bring it up next time?" which is the obvious question and the right one and I said "maybe" which is the word I use when I mean probably not.

Going to bed. Plant looks bad. I'll water it tomorrow. I'll also probably not bring it up next time.

entries.prompt: Outside of work — at home, with friends, on a walk, wherever — when did you most recently feel something soften in you this week? What were you doing, and what was the moment just before it?
entries.content:
Saturday morning. Avery was still asleep and I got up because I had to pee and then I was just up, the way you are at 7:40 on a Saturday when your body refuses to understand it's the weekend. The apartment was that specific kind of quiet where the fridge hum is the loudest thing. I stood in the kitchen for a second not really doing anything, just looking at the counter, the two mugs from last night still in the sink, the bag of coffee Avery bought from the place on the corner because I mentioned once, like in passing two months ago, that I liked it.

I made coffee. The slow way, not the fast way. Avery has this little pour-over thing that I always think is too much effort and then every time I actually use it I remember it's not, it's just five minutes of standing there watching water go through grounds. I ground the beans and the kitchen smelled like the kind of kitchen I want to live in. I boiled the water. I poured it slow, in little circles, the way Avery does it, and the way I learned to do it by watching Avery do it without ever really admitting I was learning.

And somewhere in the middle of that — the second pour, I think, when the bloom had settled and I was just watching the water go dark — something in my chest went quiet. Not happy exactly. Just quiet. Like the volume knob turned down two clicks. I noticed it the way you notice when a song you didn't realize was playing stops.

The moment just before it was — I'd been standing there with the kettle and I'd had this little flicker of, *you should be doing something, it's the weekend, you have time, you could be answering Nat's text from Tuesday, you could be looking at that course thing, you could be —* and then I just wasn't. The flicker came and went and then I was pouring water on coffee and the kitchen was warm and Avery was asleep down the hall and there was no one to be efficient for.

That's the part I keep coming back to. There was no one to perform for. Avery wasn't watching. Drew wasn't watching. Reese wasn't watching. The audience in my head had all gone home for the night and nobody had told me until just then.

I poured the coffee into the mug, the green one with the chip on the rim, and I sat at the table with it. Didn't open my phone. I had this thought, very clearly, like a sentence someone was saying to me: *this is what being okay feels like.* And then immediately after, almost embarrassed: *huh. I forgot.*

I sat there for I don't know how long. Probably ten minutes. Maybe fifteen. The light was doing the thing where it comes in low and yellow through the window over the sink. The plant — okay, the plant is not actually dead, I keep saying it's dying but it has like four leaves that are clearly fine, I've been catastrophizing the plant, the plant is doing its best — the plant was in the light. I drank the coffee. It was good. The coffee was just good. I wasn't drinking it on the way to anything. I wasn't drinking it while reading something. I was drinking it.

Then Avery shuffled out in the gray sweatshirt that's technically mine but functionally Avery's now, hair completely sideways, and said "you made the good coffee" in the voice people have before their first words have warmed up, and I said "I made the good coffee" and Avery sat down across from me and I poured the second cup and we didn't really talk for a while. Avery looked at the window. I looked at the table. It was — I don't know how to say this without it sounding like a greeting card — it was fine. It was completely fine. Nothing was being measured. Nothing was being performed. Avery wasn't asking how my week was. I wasn't editing what I would say if asked.

After a while Avery said "what do you want to do today" and I said "I don't know" and Avery said "okay" and that was the whole exchange and it didn't feel like I needed to fill it. Usually I would fill it. Usually I would already be running a list — we could go to the market, we could go on a walk, we should probably do laundry, when's the last time we — and instead I just sat there with my coffee and let the not-knowing be a thing we were both fine with.

I'm writing this Saturday night and most of the day after that was normal, I got tense again about something dumb around 4pm, I checked Slack twice for no reason, I almost ordered food and then we made eggs instead. The softness didn't last all day. I don't think it was supposed to. But I keep thinking about that fifteen minutes at the table. The coffee, the light, the plant being more alive than I'd been telling myself.

The thing I want to remember, that I'm afraid I won't remember by Monday at 9am, is that the quiet was already there. It wasn't something I had to build. I just had to not be performing for anyone, including myself, for long enough that I could hear it. Which sounds simple and is apparently extremely not.

Avery's watching something in the other room. I can hear the laugh track. I think I'm going to go sit on the couch and not bring my phone.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you wanted something for yourself that felt a little embarrassing to admit you wanted? Describe the moment the wanting showed up — where you were, what set it off, and what you did with it.
entries.content:
Wednesday, on the bus home. I was scrolling LinkedIn, which I shouldn't have been doing because LinkedIn at 6:15pm on a packed bus is a known accelerant, and someone from college — not even someone I was close to, like a person whose name I would have to think for two seconds to place — had posted about getting promoted. Senior something. With a little paragraph about gratitude and the team and the journey. And I tapped through to their profile, the way you do, and it was just — a list. A clean list of titles and companies, each one a little bigger than the last, no gaps, no detours.

And I had this lurch. This actual physical lurch, like the bus had braked but it hadn't. What I felt wasn't envy, exactly. It was more specific than that. What I felt was: I want someone to write a paragraph like that about me. Not me writing it about myself. Someone else writing it. Something where the someone else is like, "thrilled to announce" and there is my name in their post and a little reason for the thrilled.

That's the embarrassing part. Not that I want to be promoted. Wanting to be promoted is normal, everyone wants that, you can say that out loud at a dinner party. The embarrassing part is that what I actually want is the announcement. The visibility of it. I want Nat and Ben and Priya to see it in the group chat. I want my mom to screenshot it and send it to her sister. I want Drew to react to it with the rocket emoji. I want Reese to see it and have whatever the Reese version of the lurch I just had is.

I just typed that out and I want to delete it. I'm not going to delete it but I want to.

The wanting showed up so fast, on the bus, that I didn't have time to dress it up in a more acceptable outfit. Usually when I want something like this I can convert it pretty quickly into a more presentable version — *I want to grow*, *I want to do meaningful work*, *I want to feel like I'm contributing* — and those are all true, those are real, but underneath all of them is this dumber, smaller thing, which is: I want to be seen being good at something. I want a public record. I want the receipts.

I locked my phone and put it face down on my leg. Looked out the window. We were on the bridge, the part where the light at this hour does the thing on the water, and I sat there feeling found out by myself. Because the thing I'd been telling myself for months is that I'm just here to do good work and learn. Heads down. Solid. Reliable. And that's true. That's also true. But it's not the only true thing, and the bus showed me the other true thing, which is that I am extremely, somewhat shamefully, hungry for a kind of recognition I haven't earned yet and don't even know how to earn.

What I did with it is — nothing, really. I didn't do anything with it. I didn't text anyone. I didn't open a doc and start drafting some plan to make myself promotable. I just sat with it for the rest of the bus ride and tried to look at it head-on instead of sideways. Because sideways is how I usually look at it. Sideways is where I tell myself I just need to be patient, the work will speak for itself, you don't want to be the kind of person who's chasing titles. Head-on is: I do want a title. I do want it to speak for me. I'm tired of the work having to speak through me when I'm so bad at speaking for it.

I thought about what I'd want the announcement to actually say, and that was the part that got embarrassing in a different way, because I couldn't fill in the blanks. What did I do that the announcement would be celebrating? I don't know. I'd want to be promoted but I don't have a specific reason I'd be promoted. I'd want the rocket emoji but I don't have the doc that earned it. The wanting is shaped like a result with nothing underneath it.

That's not totally fair to me. I do work. The work is fine. But you don't get a thrilled-to-announce post for fine.

When I got off the bus I was still sort of carrying it. Avery was at the apartment when I got there, sitting on the couch with the laptop, and looked up and said hi and I said hi and went straight to the kitchen and got water. I didn't tell Avery about the bus. What would I even say. *Hey I had a feeling on the bus that I want to be famous in a small professional way and I noticed I haven't done anything to deserve it.* Avery would be kind about it. Avery is kind about everything. But I didn't want kind right then. I wanted to keep the wanting to myself for a little while longer, like a thing I was still figuring out the shape of.

Later I almost mentioned it. We were eating and Avery asked how work was and I had this opening where I could've said, "I had a weird thought on the bus." And I didn't. I said work was fine. Same as always. The instinct to keep the embarrassing wanting private was stronger than the instinct to share it.

I'm not sure that's bad, actually. Some things you have to hold for a minute before you let other people touch them. I think this is one of those.

What I notice, writing this — I keep wanting to end the entry by saying *and so I'm going to do X about it.* Make a plan. Pick an initiative. Be Reese. And I'm not going to. Not tonight. Tonight I just wanted to write down that I wanted it. The rocket emoji. The thrilled to announce. The paragraph with my name in it. I wanted it on the bus and I want it now and I'm going to go brush my teeth.

entries.prompt: Think about a compliment or piece of praise you got recently — from anyone, about anything. How did you receive it in the moment, and what did you do with it once you were alone?
entries.content:
Thursday, end of day, Drew said something to me that I have been turning over for about thirty hours now and I want to write it down before I sand it down any further.

We were standing by the printer, of all places. I was waiting on a thing and Drew walked up to grab something and we did the small nod and then Drew said, "Hey, by the way — the spec doc you wrapped last week. That was really clean. Like, genuinely. I forwarded it to the design folks as an example of what I want these to look like."

That was it. Two sentences. By the printer.

I said "oh — thanks, that's, yeah, glad it was useful." Glad it was useful. Like a customer service representative. My voice did this weird flat thing where it tried not to sound too pleased because sounding too pleased would be uncool, so instead I sounded like I was confirming a delivery. Drew did the half-nod, took the printout, walked off. The whole thing was maybe nine seconds.

And then I stood there at the printer with my own thing not even printing yet and I felt — okay this is the part I want to be honest about — I felt enormous. Like physically larger. For about four seconds. The compliment hit and there was this surge of, *yes, see, I do, I am, it does count.* And then almost instantly, on its heels, the second wave: *was it really that good or is Drew just being nice. Did Drew actually forward it or was that a thing people say. If Drew forwarded it to design then design has read it and what if design thought it was fine but not example-fine. What if Drew said that to Reese about something last week too and I just didn't see it.*

Four seconds of enormous, and then the picking apart started, and the picking apart took the rest of the walk back to my desk.

I sat down and I couldn't look at my screen for a minute. I had this stupid impulse to text someone — Avery, Nat, anyone — and say *Drew said my doc was clean, Drew forwarded it to design.* I wanted to put it somewhere outside my head so it could exist as a real thing instead of a thing I was about to dissolve. I didn't text anyone. I almost did and then I thought, if I tell Avery now Avery will be happy for me and then I'll have to be happy back and I'll have to perform the appropriate amount of happy and the performing will use up the actual happy and I'll be left with nothing. So I sat with it. Or I tried to. What I actually did was open the spec doc and reread it, looking for the part that was example-good, trying to figure out what specifically Drew meant. Was it the structure. Was it the way I'd flagged the open questions. Was it just that it was on time. I wanted to find the specific sentence Drew had liked so I could do that sentence again on purpose next time. Which is — I was already trying to convert the compliment into a recipe before I had even let it be a compliment.

I closed the laptop early-ish, like 5:30. On the bus home I let myself replay it once, properly. *That was really clean. Genuinely.* The word genuinely is the part I keep landing on. Drew doesn't say genuinely a lot. Drew is sparing with adverbs. The genuinely is doing real work in that sentence. I tried to just hold that and not pick at it.

Got home. Avery was on the couch. I said "Drew said something nice about my work today" and Avery looked up and smiled and said "yeah? what?" and I told it. The two sentences. The forwarded-to-design part. Avery said, "Babe, that's a big deal," and I did the thing where I went, "I mean, I don't know, it's just one doc," and Avery just looked at me. Just looked. Didn't argue. And I felt myself caught. Because I had immediately tried to deflate it and Avery had simply declined to deflate it with me. After a second I said, "okay yeah, it's a nice thing, I'm letting it be a nice thing," and Avery said "good" and went back to the laptop and that was that.

Later, brushing my teeth, I caught myself in the mirror and I had this flicker of, *if Drew thinks the doc is example-good then maybe I'm not coasting, maybe the work is actually registering,* and then immediately, *don't get cocky, one printer compliment doesn't undo the rocket emoji you didn't get,* and then I noticed I was negotiating with a compliment in my own bathroom at 10:45pm and I just kind of laughed at myself, foam in my mouth, and spit.

What did I do with it once I was alone. I tried to make it into evidence. I tried to make it into a recipe. I tried to deflate it. I tried to weigh it against Reese's doc and see if it was the same weight or less. I told one person and let one person keep it bigger than I was going to keep it. And I think — and I want to say this carefully because I don't fully trust it yet — I think a small piece of it is still intact. Not the enormous part. That went pretty fast. But underneath, a smaller, steadier thing that says: Drew read the doc and Drew thought it was clean. That happened. That's a fact in the world now. It doesn't mean I'm safe and it doesn't mean I'm Reese and it doesn't mean the next thing will land. It just means that one thing landed.

The genuinely is still doing work. I'm going to let it.

entries.prompt: Tell me about a time recently when you were alone and caught yourself doing something the way someone else does it — a habit, a phrasing, a gesture you've absorbed from a person in your life. What were you doing, and whose was it?
entries.content:
Sunday afternoon, by myself in the apartment, Avery was out doing the grocery thing we usually do together but I'd said go without me because I had this vague idea of cleaning and instead I was standing in the kitchen eating cold rice out of the container with a fork. Which is fine. That's not the thing.

The thing is I was on the phone with my mom. She'd called, of course she'd called, Sunday at like 2pm is her window, and I'd picked up because I felt guilty about not picking up last week. And she was telling me about my aunt's knee surgery in detail I did not need, and I was doing the responses — *mm, oh no, yeah, mm* — and at some point I realized I was doing them with this specific cadence that was not mine. It was Avery's. It was exactly Avery's.

Avery has this way of being on the phone with their own mom where they make these little affirming noises that aren't agreement, exactly, they're more like — *I'm here, keep going, I'm not going anywhere* — and the noises have a rhythm. A soft *mm* and then a slightly higher *mhm* and then a *yeah* with a little dip in it. I have heard Avery do this hundreds of times from the other room, sitting on the couch, while I'm cooking or whatever. I have absorbed it without ever deciding to.

And there I was, leaning against the counter, fork in hand, doing Avery's phone voice with my mother. My mother, who I usually talk to in this slightly clipped, slightly impatient way where I'm waiting for the call to be over. And I wasn't doing that. I was doing the *mm, mhm, yeah-with-a-dip* thing and my mom was just — talking. More than usual. Easier than usual. She told me about the knee, and then she told me about a thing with the neighbor's dog, and then she told me about how she'd been trying a new recipe and it hadn't worked, and normally by the dog story I'd be making an exit, but I wasn't, because Avery's noises don't make exits, Avery's noises make space.

I noticed it somewhere in the middle of the recipe story. The recognition was so specific it almost knocked me out of the call. *That's not how I sound on the phone. That's how Avery sounds on the phone.* And then a second behind it: *my mom is talking more because of it.*

I kept doing it. I didn't switch back. That felt like it would be weird, like switching mid-song. So I just — kept being on the phone the way Avery is on the phone, and my mom kept telling me things, and at the end she said, "this was a good call, honey," and I said "yeah, it was," and after we hung up I stood there with the rice container still in my hand thinking about how my mom had said *good call* with this little surprised note, like she'd been expecting the usual and gotten something else.

I have absorbed Avery in other ways and I know I have. The pour-over, obviously, that's the one I've already admitted to myself. The way I say "for sure" now instead of "yeah totally" — that's Avery's. The way I tilt my head when I'm reading something carefully — I caught myself doing that on the bus last week and felt briefly haunted. The way I say "what do you need" when someone seems off, instead of "are you okay," which is what I used to say. That one I stole on purpose, almost, because I noticed how much better it worked.

But the phone voice. The phone voice I didn't know I had taken. The phone voice I took without meaning to and apparently use only when I'm not paying attention to myself, which is maybe the most damning evidence that I've taken it, because the things you do on autopilot are the things that have actually become yours.

I don't know how I feel about it. Part of me — and this is the dumb part — part of me felt this little flicker of *am I losing myself, am I just becoming a person-shaped echo of Avery,* like there's some original me that's getting painted over. But that's stupid. The original me had a clipped impatient phone voice with my mother that made every call worse for both of us. If the original me is being painted over by a version that lets my mom tell me about the recipe that didn't work, I don't actually want the original me back.

The other thing is — Avery learned the noises from somewhere too. Probably from Avery's mom, or Avery's dad, or some friend of Avery's I don't know. Nobody invents how to be on the phone. You catch it from the people who were on the phone near you. I just happened to catch this one late, at 24, from a person I sleep next to, instead of as a kid from the people who raised me.

What I'm sitting with is — I have spent a lot of energy this year worried about what Drew thinks of me and what Reese is doing and whether my work registers, and meanwhile, in the quiet domestic background, I've been turning into a slightly different person. Not in any way I planned. Just by being in the room with someone whose noises I apparently wanted. Whose rhythm I let in.

Avery got home with the groceries about an hour later and I helped put them away and I didn't say anything about the phone call. Didn't say *I sounded like you today and my mom liked it.* That felt like it would make the thing self-conscious in a way that would ruin it. Some things you have to keep absorbing in private. I did, when Avery was reaching for something on the top shelf, look at the back of Avery's neck for a second and feel briefly, embarrassingly grateful. For the noises. For having been close enough to catch them.

Then we put the eggs in the fridge and Avery asked what I wanted for dinner and I said "I don't know" and Avery said "okay" and I let the not-knowing sit there, which, also — also Avery's.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel relief that something got cancelled or fell through — a plan, a meeting, a phone call you were dreading? Walk me through the moment you found out it wasn't happening, and what you did with the time instead.
entries.content:
Tuesday night, around 8:50, I was on the couch already in the soft pants and Nat's name lit up on my phone. We had a call scheduled for 9. We'd been trying to schedule this call for like three weeks. The kind of scheduling where one person suggests Thursday and the other person says oh I can't Thursday, what about Sunday, and Sunday turns into next Tuesday and next Tuesday turns into a calendar invite that sits there glowing for six days like a small homework assignment.

I had been low-grade dreading it since lunch. Not because I didn't want to talk to Nat. I do want to talk to Nat. I miss Nat in this specific way where I can feel the shape of the missing but I can't always make myself pick up the phone about it. The dread was about the performance of the call. The catching up. The *so what's new*. The part where Nat would ask about work and I'd have to compress eight months of Drew and Reese and the spec doc into something tellable, and then Nat would tell me about Nat's thing and I would have to be the right amount of impressed and the right amount of asking-follow-up-questions, and the whole call would have the shape of a thing being maintained rather than a thing being lived.

So when Nat's text came through and it said *omg I am so sorry, can we push?? today has been a disaster, I'll explain when we talk* — I felt this wave of relief that was so immediate and so total that I actually said "oh thank god" out loud to nobody. Avery was in the other room. The plant heard me. That was it.

I texted back *omg no worries, totally, whenever works* and added a heart, because I am a person who adds a heart to soften the relief I'm feeling. And then I sat there with the phone and I had this immediate second thing, right behind the relief, which was — wait. Wait. I was relieved that I didn't have to talk to Nat. Nat. Who I lived with junior year. Who held my hair the time I had food poisoning at that wedding. Who knows the names of every person I dated in college. I was relieved.

That sat badly. It sat badly for about a minute and then I just let it sit, because the relief was the relief and pretending it wasn't there wasn't going to make it not there.

What I did with the time. Okay. I want to be honest about this because it is the most damning part. What I did with the time was I made myself a second cup of the tea Avery bought, the green one with the lemon, and I sat back down on the couch and I opened my phone and I scrolled. I scrolled for like forty minutes. Instagram, then twitter-equivalent, then back to instagram, then a real estate app I don't know why I have, then back to instagram. I watched a video of a dog meeting a baby. I watched a video of someone making a galette. I watched a video of a woman explaining why her promotion timeline was actually ahead of schedule. That one I closed fast.

I did not read. I did not call my mom back. I did not water the plant, even though the plant is right there, even though I keep telling myself I'll water the plant. I did not text Nat back to actually reschedule. I just — sank. I sank into the couch and I sank into the phone and I let the saved hour become nothing.

And the thing I'm noticing tonight, writing this on Wednesday at the kitchen table because I didn't write last night, is that this is the pattern. I dread something, the thing falls through, I feel relief, and then I waste the time the relief gave me. Like the relief itself is the reward and I don't actually need the time for anything. I just needed to not do the thing.

Which is — what does that mean. I don't know what that means. I think it means I am not actually short on time. I think it means I am short on something else and I keep blaming time for it. I had an hour. I had a clean unscheduled hour. I could have actually called Nat back. The call I was dreading was right there, available, and Nat would have picked up — Nat texted me, Nat is clearly around — and instead of using the relief to be brave I used it to be small.

The other thing I keep coming back to is Nat's text. *Today has been a disaster, I'll explain when we talk.* Nat had a hard day. Nat was reaching out, in the sense that Nat was canceling but also flagging that there was a story, that there was something to tell. And I let it slide off because I was so glad to be off the hook. I didn't even ask *are you okay.* I said *omg no worries* and added a heart and went and watched a galette video.

I want to text Nat right now. It's 9:47 on Wednesday. I could text Nat right now and say *hey, how was the disaster, want to actually talk this weekend, I'll work around you.* I could do that in literally thirty seconds. The phone is right here.

I am going to. I'm going to do it before I close this. Because otherwise I'll convert this whole entry into a recipe — *next time I'll be better, next time I'll use the hour* — and there isn't a next time, there's just Nat, who texted me yesterday, who I have not responded to with anything other than a non-answer.

Okay. Sending it. *hey — how was the disaster?? this weekend any good? I can do saturday or sunday, whatever's easier.* Sent.

That took eleven seconds. Eleven seconds. I had forty minutes.

The plant. I'm going to water the plant.

entries.prompt: Last week, when did someone ask you for something — a favor, your time, an opinion — and you felt yourself calculating before you answered? Walk me through what was running through your head between the ask and your response.
entries.content:
Friday afternoon, like 4:15, Reese leaned over the half-wall thing between our desks and said, "Hey, do you have a sec — could you take a look at the second half of this thing before I send it? Just a gut check."

A gut check.

The pause between Reese asking and me answering was, in real time, maybe two seconds. Inside the two seconds was a whole weather system.

First thing through my head: Reese is asking *me*. Reese, who made the nine-page handoff doc, who got the rocket emoji within ninety seconds, is asking me for a gut check. There was this little pulse of — pride is the wrong word, it's smaller than that, it's like getting tapped on the shoulder by the kid you'd assumed didn't know your name. I felt it before I had time to be embarrassed about feeling it.

Second thing: what does Reese want from me, actually. Is this a real ask or is this a politeness ask. Is Reese going to take what I say and use it or is Reese going to nod and send the thing as written and I'll have spent forty minutes being thorough for nothing. And under that, smaller — am I qualified. Reese is six months ahead of me. What am I going to catch that Reese hasn't already caught. What if I read it and have nothing to say. What if I read it and the only thing I can think to say is something dumb and Reese clocks that I'm out of my depth.

Third thing, which arrived faster than the second: it's 4:15. I have my own thing I told Drew I'd send before EOD. The thing is not in good shape. The thing is in the shape where if I sit down and grind for the next hour and forty-five it will be done, and if I take a forty-minute detour through Reese's doc it will not be done, or it will be done badly. And Drew said *send it by EOD Friday* in the voice that wasn't urgent but also wasn't optional.

Fourth thing, basically simultaneous with the third: if I say no, Reese will think I'm not a team player. If I say no, Reese will not ask me again. If I say no, Reese will ask someone else and that someone else will get the small tap on the shoulder and I will be back to being the person nobody asks. If I say no, Drew might at some point hear that Reese asked around for a second pair of eyes and I declined.

Fifth thing: but if I say yes and don't get my own thing done, Drew will notice that. Drew will notice that more than Drew will notice me helping Reese, because the thing for Drew is a thing for Drew and helping Reese is a thing for Reese, and the math of who I am being good for is not balanced.

Sixth thing, and this is the one I want to write down because I almost didn't notice it noticing: a flicker of *this is a chance to read Reese's work without it looking weird.* To finally see, up close, what Reese actually does. To find out if the gap between us is as big as it feels or if Reese also has a hand-wavy paragraph in the middle. That flicker had a small ugly shape and I kept moving past it.

Seventh thing, more diffuse: tired. I was just tired. It was Friday at 4:15 and I had been pushing all week and someone was asking me for something and the part of me that runs the calculator was running it slowly because the batteries were low. I felt myself wanting to say yes just to make the asking stop. Just to get to the part where I had agreed to something and could go back to my screen.

So between the ask and the answer, in two seconds, I ran: ego, doubt, the EOD clock, the Drew calculus, the Reese calculus, the curiosity, and the tiredness. And what came out of my mouth was — "Yeah, totally, send it over, I'll take a look in a bit."

Yeah totally. Send it over. I'll take a look in a bit.

The "in a bit" was the only piece of self-preservation I managed. *In a bit* meaning, I am going to try to do my own thing first and then look at yours, which is the version of yes that I hoped would let me keep both. Reese said "you're a star, no rush, anytime before Monday is fine," and I felt this little drop because — anytime before Monday. It wasn't urgent. I had assumed it was urgent. I had taken on urgency that wasn't there. I could have said *let me get my thing out the door first and I'll look at it Monday morning fresh* and Reese would have said yes and the whole thing would have been clean.

But I didn't ask. I didn't ask because asking would have meant pausing the calculator long enough to gather information, and the calculator had already spit out an answer.

What I did then. I worked on my thing until 5:45, sent it to Drew at 5:51, opened Reese's doc on the bus home which was a stupid place to read it because the bus was packed and I couldn't focus, and then read it again at the kitchen table after dinner while Avery watched something on the couch. It was good. It was good in the specific way Reese's stuff is good, which is structurally, the bones of it. I had two real comments and one nitpick. I sent them at 9:30 with a "hope these are useful, feel free to ignore." Reese replied within four minutes with "these are great, thank you!! the second one especially — you're right." The exclamation points were doing real work.

I sat there with the phone and felt — okay. Felt useful. Felt like Reese had asked a real question and I had given a real answer and Reese had taken the answer. The calculator had been running the whole time and the actual transaction had been small and fine.

What I keep landing on is — I said yes before I knew what I was saying yes to. The "anytime before Monday" was sitting there the whole time and I never asked. I bought urgency on credit. I do that. I do that a lot.

Next time someone asks. Just — *when do you need it by.* Five words. I'm writing them down here so maybe I'll remember them on Monday.

Going to bed. Plant is, somehow, fine.

entries.prompt: There's a kind of tiredness that isn't about sleep — it's about having been a particular version of yourself for too long in a row. When did you last feel that kind of tired, and what were you in the middle of when you noticed it?
entries.content:
Thursday, around 3pm, in the conference room with the bad chairs. We were forty minutes into a working session that should have been twenty, and someone — I won't even name who, it doesn't matter, it was the person who always does this — was relitigating a decision we'd already made on Monday. And I was nodding. I was doing the thing where my face is engaged and my pen is moving on the notebook and I am making the small affirming sound that says *I'm with you, I'm tracking,* and inside my head there was just static. Not thoughts. Static.

I noticed it when Drew glanced at me across the table for backup — Drew had a position, the position was the one we'd already agreed on, and Drew wanted me to chime in. And I opened my mouth and said, "Yeah, I think we landed on the second option for a reason, and I think that reason still holds," which is a fine sentence, which is a sentence I am proud of in the abstract, and as I was saying it I had this out-of-body moment where I thought *who is talking. who is this person. why does her voice sound like that.*

It wasn't even a bad voice. It was the voice. The work voice. The slightly-lower-than-my-actual-voice, slightly-more-measured, slightly-more-finished voice. The voice that says "I think we landed on" instead of "we already decided this." The voice that knows not to roll its eyes. The voice that has been on since 8:45 this morning and has, at this point, been on for — I tried to count — basically four straight days. Monday meeting. Tuesday 1:1 with Drew. Tuesday afternoon thing with the design folks. Wednesday all-hands. Wednesday the printer chat with someone from another team that turned into twenty minutes of standing politely. Thursday morning standup. Thursday this.

Four days of being the version of me that has the right voice and the right posture and the right amount of nodding. Four days of running the calculator in the background. Four days of *kind of similar to what Reese said, basically* never happening again, because I will not, I will not let it happen again, which means every sentence has been pre-cleared by a small panicked editor before it leaves my mouth.

And in the conference room I just hit a wall. Not a dramatic wall. I didn't say anything weird. I didn't get up. The wall was internal and it felt like — you know when your laptop has too many tabs open and the fan kicks on and everything gets slow and warm. Like that. Like the fan in my head turned on and I was the laptop.

The meeting kept going. I kept nodding. Drew said something, the relitigating person said something back, someone else proposed a compromise that was just the original decision in different words, and we all agreed to the compromise, and we ended ten minutes over. I walked back to my desk and sat down and stared at my screen and could not — I could not produce a single useful action. I opened slack. I closed slack. I opened the doc I was supposed to be working on and I read the same sentence four times and the words stayed words, they didn't become meaning. The fan was still going. The fan was loud.

What I was in the middle of, when I noticed it — that's the prompt. I was in the middle of being competent in a meeting. I was in the middle of being the person who has the right take. Which is the version of me I have been most carefully constructing for the last eight months. And the noticing was: I am so tired of her. I am so tired of being her for this many hours a day. She is fine. She is doing okay. Drew likes her. The spec doc was clean, *genuinely.* But she has been on every single day this week and I have nowhere to put the her-ness down.

I stayed at my desk till 5:40 doing essentially nothing useful and then I gave up and went home. On the bus I didn't open my phone. I didn't have the bandwidth to scroll. Scrolling, I realized, also requires a version of me — the version that reacts, the version that has opinions about the galette, the version that closes the promotion video fast. I didn't have her either. I just looked out the window. The fan in my head slowly stopped.

When I got home Avery said "hey, how was it" and I said "I'm just — I'm fried, can I have like twenty minutes," and Avery said "yeah of course," and I went and lay on the bed with my shoes still on and stared at the ceiling. And the relief of not having to be a particular shape was so complete it almost felt like sadness. Like I had been holding something up with both arms and I'd put it down and now my arms didn't know what to do.

That's the kind of tired I mean. Not sleepy. Not even burned out, exactly, that word feels too big. More like — the muscles you use to be a specific person are sore. The smile-at-the-printer muscle. The right-amount-of-engaged-in-the-meeting muscle. The thread-the-needle-with-Drew muscle. The not-be-weird-in-front-of-Reese muscle. They all live on the same side of my face apparently and they were all twitching by Thursday at 3.

I don't know what to do with this. I can't not be her. Being her is the job. Being her is a thing I want to be, I think, most of the time — I built her on purpose, I am still building her. But four days in a row, with no off-ramp, and I am so depleted I cannot read a sentence on my own screen.

Avery came in after a bit and just lay down next to me and didn't say anything. After a while I said "I don't want to be on for anyone for the rest of the night" and Avery said "okay" and that was it. We ordered food. I let myself.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been meaning to do for yourself — not urgent, not for anyone else — that keeps getting pushed to next week? Tell me what it is and what last week's version of pushing it looked like.
entries.content:
The thing is the gym. Or — not the gym, exactly. A gym. Any gym. I haven't been to one in like fourteen months and I keep saying I'm going to start again and I don't.

This isn't a New Year thing. I'm not trying to lose weight. My body is fine, my body is doing its job, my body carries me to the bus and back. The wanting is more specific than that and harder to say out loud. I miss being in my body in a way that isn't me staring at a screen with my shoulders up by my ears. I miss the part where you do a hard thing for forty minutes and then you walk out and the world is quieter. I had that in college. I had it for like six months after college, even, I had a little routine where I went three times a week to the place near my old apartment and I knew which treadmill didn't squeak. And then I moved here and started the job and said *I'll find a place once I get settled* and I never got settled, or I got settled in every other way except this one, and the not-doing of it has become its own little fixture in the apartment, like the plant.

Last week's version of pushing it. Okay. Sunday afternoon I sat down with my laptop and a coffee and I thought, today I'm going to actually pick a gym. I had two tabs open. The chain place that's six blocks from the apartment, which is the obvious answer. And the smaller one near the bus stop on the way home, which is more expensive but means I could just stop on the way back from work, which is the thing I keep telling myself would make me actually go.

I read both pages. I read the membership pages. I read the FAQ. I read the *how to cancel* page on the chain one because that's a thing I do, I research the exit before I've even gone in. I opened the smaller one's class schedule. I closed the smaller one's class schedule. I went back to the chain one. I put their address into maps to time the walk. Six minutes. Six minutes is a number that does not have any excuses inside it. Six minutes is *yes, you could go.*

And then I did not sign up. I closed both tabs. I told myself I would do it Tuesday. I have no idea why Tuesday. Tuesday was just a word that meant *not now.*

Tuesday came and went and I did not sign up. Wednesday I was tired from the printer-compliment day and signing up for a gym felt like a thing for a more put-together person. Thursday I was the laptop with the fan on, that whole conference room thing, and I was not signing up for anything. Friday I forgot it was a thing I'd been thinking about. Saturday I went to the farmer's market with Avery and felt vaguely active and let that count.

So that was last week's pushing. Two open tabs, closed. *Tuesday.* Then the week.

The thing I want to say about it that I haven't said yet is — I think I'm scared of starting badly. That's the actual blocker. It's not money, the chain place is like forty bucks a month, I order more food than that in a bad week. It's not time, six minutes, six minutes. It's that I used to be in shape, in the very low bar way that someone in their early twenties can be in shape without trying, and I am not in that shape anymore, and the first time I go I am going to find out exactly how not-in-that-shape I am. I am going to get on a treadmill and I am going to be winded faster than I expect and I am going to have to look at that, in a room full of mirrors, with strangers around, and the version of me who built the careful work voice is also the version of me who does not want to be witnessed being bad at something I used to be okay at.

That's so dumb. I just typed it and it's so dumb. Nobody at the gym is looking at me. The people at the gym are looking at themselves in the mirrors, which is what mirrors at gyms are for. The number of people who will clock my pace on a treadmill is zero. I know this. I knew this on Sunday too. I closed the tabs anyway.

What I think is also true — and I want to be careful here — is that this is a thing that nobody is going to give me a rocket emoji for. Drew is not going to react to me going to the gym. Reese will not know. Avery will be supportive in the unobtrusive way Avery is supportive about everything, which is great, which is also not the kind of feedback that makes the calculator inside me agree to do a thing. The going to the gym is genuinely just for me. The benefit lives entirely inside my own body, which is a place I have apparently stopped trusting as a real place where good things can happen. If a thing isn't visible to Drew, the part of me that runs the day says *later.* And later means never. I keep noticing that the things I push to next week are the things that don't have anyone watching.

Okay. So. The chain place. Six minutes. Forty bucks. I'm going to sign up tomorrow. I'm not going to sign up tonight because if I say I'll sign up tonight I won't, I'll be too tired by 10, and then I'll have failed at the small version and it'll get bigger. Tomorrow morning, with coffee. The good coffee. Pour-over. I'll do the pour-over and while it's blooming I'll fill out the form. That's the deal.

I'm not going to go this week necessarily. I'm just going to sign up. The signing up is the thing. After that I can push the going to next week, which is fine, which is allowed, but I will have a membership, which means the not-going will at least cost me something, which is maybe what I need. A small ongoing charge against my account that says *you said you wanted this.*

Plant is fine. I watered it Tuesday. Apparently I can do a thing if it's small and lives on the windowsill and only needs me once a week. Building from there.

entries.prompt: Why does it feel embarrassing to want the gym thing — or the quiet-after-a-hard-thing thing — for yourself? Write about where that flavor of embarrassment shows up elsewhere too, not just around this.
entries.content:
The embarrassment about the gym thing is — okay, let me try to get at it. It's not embarrassing to want to be fit. That's a normal sentence. People say that out loud all the time. *I want to get back in shape.* Nobody flinches. The embarrassing part is the second layer. The wanting underneath the wanting. Which is: I want to feel the quiet after a hard thing. I want forty minutes where the only voice in my head is the one going *one more, one more,* instead of the seventeen voices I usually run. I want to walk out of a building with my legs shaky and my brain finally turned off and stand on the sidewalk just being a body for a minute.

That's the embarrassing one. Saying I want to *be quiet inside myself* feels like a thing a person says in a wellness ad. It feels — soft. Like I'm asking for something I haven't earned. Like *oh, you, with the spec doc and the apartment and the partner and the job, you also need inner peace? cute.*

And the deeper thing, the part that makes me want to not type this — I'm embarrassed that I am a person who needs to *go somewhere* and *do an activity* in order to be quiet. I am embarrassed that I can't just be quiet. That my baseline isn't quiet and I have to buy quiet with forty bucks a month and a treadmill. The version of me I want to be doesn't need a transaction to find the quiet. The version of me I want to be just has it. Saturday morning at the kitchen table with the coffee and the light — that version. That version doesn't need a gym. That version is already there.

But the Saturday morning version is rare. The Saturday morning version showed up by accident, once, in eight months. I cannot reproduce her on demand. So I am, apparently, the kind of person who needs to pay a chain gym to manufacture for me what one good Saturday morning produced for free.

That's the flavor. *I shouldn't need this. I should be able to just have it. The fact that I have to engineer it means something is wrong with me.*

And once I name the flavor I can taste it everywhere.

It's the flavor when I want to ask Avery for reassurance. *Tell me I'm okay.* I never say it. I never say it because saying it would mean I am a person who needs to be told. The version of me I want to be doesn't need to be told. The version of me I want to be just knows. So when the wanting shows up — and it shows up a lot, like at the kitchen counter when Avery says *I'm sure it wasn't as bad as you think* and I want so badly to ask *say more, say it again, say it specifically* — I swallow it. The asking is the embarrassment. The asking proves I'm not the version.

It's the flavor when I want Drew to tell me I'm doing fine. *Anything else on your mind.* Drew literally asked. And I couldn't say *yes, please reassure me, please tell me I'm tracking,* because needing that is — the same flavor. People who are good at the job don't need to ask if they're good at the job. They just are. So if I ask, I am revealing that I am not.

It's the flavor when I miss Nat and Ben and Priya. I want to text the group chat and say *I miss you, I feel far away.* I want to say it plain. And I don't because — needing the friend group to be active is embarrassing. Functional adults don't need their college friends to text every day. Functional adults have moved on. So I let the chat thin and I feel the missing privately and I don't admit out loud that I want it, because the wanting is the thing I'm ashamed of, not the not-having.

It's the flavor when I want my mom to be proud of me about something specific. Not the general *we love you, we're proud of you* she does at the end of every call. Something specific. *Mom, I wrote this thing at work and my boss forwarded it to other people as an example.* I haven't told her. I haven't told her because telling her would be asking for the specific proud, and asking for it would be — the flavor.

There's a pattern here that I'm only seeing now, typing it. The embarrassment isn't about the thing. It's about the *needing* of the thing. The wanting itself. I think I have, somewhere along the way, decided that a competent adult is a person whose wants are quiet and self-contained. Who doesn't need to be told. Who doesn't need to be reassured. Who doesn't need to engineer the quiet because the quiet is already there. Who doesn't need to ask because the asking is the failure.

And so any want that exceeds that quiet self-contained allotment is — embarrassing. Loud. Childish. *Too much.*

Which is funny because I know, I *know,* that the people I admire most are not the ones with no needs. Avery has needs. Avery says them out loud. Avery says *can you put your phone down* and *I want to do nothing tonight* and *that hurt my feelings* and the world does not end. Avery does not lose status by needing things. Avery gets needs met by saying them. This is not a secret. This is just how it works. I have watched it work for eight months and I have not, somehow, generalized.

The gym is a thing I want for myself. The reassurance is a thing I want from Avery. The *am I tracking* is a thing I want from Drew. The group chat being warm again is a thing I want from Nat and Ben and Priya. The specific proud is a thing I want from my mom. Each one of them, by itself, I can almost say. All of them together, listed like that, it looks like — a lot. It looks like a person with a lot of needs.

Maybe I am a person with a lot of needs. Maybe that's allowed. Maybe the embarrassment is just the price of being 24 and not having figured out yet that wanting things is what you're supposed to do.

I'm going to sign up for the gym tomorrow. With the pour-over. That part is still the deal. And I'm going to try, this week, to say one want out loud to Avery, even a small one. Not as an experiment. Just as practice. *I want you to tell me the spec doc thing was a big deal one more time.* Something like that. Small. Specific. Embarrassing.

The plant has a new leaf. I noticed yesterday. I didn't tell anyone. I'm telling here.

entries.prompt: When did you last laugh at something — actually laugh, not the polite version — and what was happening just before? Tell me who you were with, or whether you were alone, and what the rest of that day looked like around it.
entries.content:
Saturday afternoon, like 2:30, on the couch with Avery, both of us still in whatever we'd slept in. We'd had a slow morning and we'd done basically nothing and it was that specific kind of Saturday where the day has not asked anything of you and you are not sure whether to be grateful or guilty, so you mostly just sit.

Avery was scrolling something on the phone and started narrating a video without showing it to me. Avery does this — describes things instead of just handing me the phone. I think it's because Avery has decided I look at my phone too much, which is true. So Avery was saying, "Okay so there's this woman, and she's at a wedding, and she's giving a toast, and — wait, no, hold on, she's the officiant, I think she's the officiant — and she has the script, and she reaches for the script, and —" and then Avery just started laughing. Not at the video. At the description. At Avery's own bad description of the video. Avery was laughing too hard to finish the sentence and was making this wheezing sound and waving the phone around, and the phone was face down on Avery's chest now because Avery had forgotten what the phone was for.

And I started laughing because Avery was laughing, and then I was laughing because Avery looked stupid laughing, hair in face, eyes squeezed shut, that little wheeze. And then Avery looked at me and saw me laughing and laughed harder, and I laughed harder, and we were just doing the thing where two people are laughing at each other laughing and the original cause has dissolved completely. I don't know what was on the video. I never found out. I still don't know. Avery never showed me. The video is, to me, a permanently unknown video that made my partner make a sound like a kettle.

At some point I was actually crying. Like — tears. I had to wipe my face with the sleeve of the sweatshirt. My stomach hurt. I haven't laughed like that in — I genuinely don't know how long. Months, easy. Maybe longer. I had this thought, mid-laugh, which is a weird place to have a thought, that the laugh was using muscles I had forgotten about. Like the smile-at-the-printer muscle's opposite. A muscle that doesn't care about being witnessed correctly. A muscle that just goes.

What was happening just before — that's the prompt. Just before, we had been quiet. Avery was on the phone, I was looking at the ceiling, the radiator was making its noise. I had been having a low-grade thought about whether I should be doing something productive, which is the Saturday afternoon thought, the one that arrives at exactly 2:15 every Saturday like a calendar invite. *You could go to the gym.* (I did sign up. I have not gone yet. The membership is a small charge I am paying to a building I have not entered. As predicted.) *You could text Priya back.* *You could clean the bathroom.* I had been low-key cycling through these and not doing any of them.

And then Avery laughed at Avery's own narration and the whole thing just — got knocked over. The little stack of *should*s I had been balancing fell off the table.

After we calmed down — and it took a while, there were aftershocks, every time one of us almost stopped the other one would do a little wheeze and we'd start again — Avery wiped eyes and said, "I have no idea why that was so funny," and I said, "me neither," and we sat there sort of hiccupping. Avery put the phone down on the coffee table, face down, and didn't pick it up again for the rest of the afternoon. I noticed that. I noticed Avery noticing that we'd had a real moment and choosing to not let the phone come back into it.

The rest of the day was — soft. We didn't do much. Avery made grilled cheese around 4, which is Avery's emotional support food, and we ate them on the couch and watched something neither of us was really watching. I texted Priya back, actually. Just *hey, miss you, how's things,* nothing fancy. Priya wrote back within an hour with a picture of her cat doing something and a long voice memo about a guy she's seeing that I listened to twice because I wanted to be the person who listened twice. I didn't go to the gym. I didn't clean the bathroom. I did the dishes from breakfast, eventually, because Avery was being patient about them in the way Avery is patient where I can feel the patience. That counts.

What I keep coming back to, writing this Sunday morning at the table — the laugh wasn't about anything. There was no joke. There was no clever thing. It was just Avery losing it in a normal way and me getting carried along. The laughter was free. It cost nothing and required nothing of me. I didn't have to be on for it. I didn't have to perform the appropriate amount of amused. The performance muscle wasn't even invited to that party. It happened in the part of my face the work voice doesn't reach.

And I think that's the thing. The Saturday morning quiet, the laugh on the couch, the phone going face down — these are happening in a room the work voice does not enter. Avery is, I am realizing, the door to that room. Or one of the doors. The door I have most often, lately. And I keep being surprised by the room every time I find it, like I haven't built a map yet. Like I keep stumbling into it and going *oh right, this exists, hi.*

Avery is at the store. I can hear the radiator. There's coffee left in the pot. I'm going to drink it before it gets cold. The plant has the new leaf and a second new leaf, which I noticed this morning, and which I have not told Avery about yet because I want to wait until Avery sees it on Avery's own.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel a flash of irritation at someone close to you — small enough that you didn't say anything — and what was the shape of it? Walk me through the moment, and what you did with it after.
entries.content:
Tuesday evening, Avery was telling me about something that had happened at work, and I was making the noises — Avery's noises, actually, I'm aware of this now, the *mm, mhm, yeah-with-a-dip* — and we were on the couch and I was half-watching Avery talk and half-watching the spot on the wall where the paint is doing that thing. And Avery said, mid-story, "you know how I am about that kind of thing," and I felt this small sharp flick of — no. No, I don't, actually. Tell me. Don't assume I know.

It was tiny. It was so tiny. The flick lasted maybe a second and a half. But it was there, and it had a specific shape, and the shape was: *you think I know you better than I do and I am quietly ashamed about how little of you I have actually been holding onto lately.*

Because here's the thing. I don't know how Avery is about that kind of thing. Not really. I might have known eight months ago when we were in the part of the relationship where you ask a lot of questions and store the answers like they're going on a test. But somewhere in the last few months I stopped storing. Not on purpose. I have just been so full of Drew and Reese and the spec doc and the calculator that the part of my brain that used to file things about Avery has been running at reduced capacity. And Avery said *you know how I am* and assumed I'd been keeping up, and the flick was — partly *don't assume,* partly *please don't find out I haven't been.*

I didn't say anything. I did the *yeah-with-a-dip* and Avery kept going and I tracked the rest of the story with extra attention, which is the guilty version of listening, the version where you're trying to make up for the previous five minutes by overcorrecting on the next five. By the end of the story I had no idea what Avery had originally been irritated about at work, because I'd been busy auditing my own attention instead of paying any.

Avery finished, looked at me, said "anyway," and got up to get water. And I sat there on the couch with the irritation already cooling into something else. Because the irritation wasn't really at Avery. Avery hadn't done anything. Avery had said a normal thing partners say to each other — *you know how I am about that* — which is a sentence that assumes intimacy, which is a sentence couples earn the right to say, and Avery had earned it, eight months in, fair and square. The irritation was at being caught not having held up my end. Avery was operating on the assumption that we were both filing. I had stopped filing. The flick was the gap between those two facts being briefly visible.

I want to be honest. There was also a smaller, uglier piece. *You know how I am about that kind of thing* is the kind of sentence that, when I'm tired, lands like — *of course you should know, I'm the more knowable one, I am the one with consistent reactions, you are the chaotic one whose moods I track.* Which is probably not at all what Avery meant. Avery meant nothing by it. Avery was just talking. But there's a way I sometimes hear, in the easy confidence of Avery's *you know how I am,* this small implication that Avery is a settled thing and I am not, and it pricks. Even when nobody is pricking on purpose. Even when the only person doing the pricking is me, with a sentence Avery threw away.

What I did with it after. Nothing dramatic. I went and got water too, in the kitchen, behind Avery, and put my chin on Avery's shoulder for a second the way I do, and Avery leaned back into it the way Avery does, and the flick was gone, or mostly gone. We watched something. I made an effort, the rest of the night, to actually pay attention when Avery talked. Not in a guilty way. Just in a — *okay, file this, this is a thing about Avery you'd want to know later* — way. Avery mentioned a coworker by name, a name I had heard before but couldn't have placed, and I asked a question about the coworker, and Avery told me, and I tried to actually hear it. The way I would hear something Drew said.

Which is a sentence I don't love writing. *The way I would hear something Drew said.* Because that's the disparity, isn't it. I am storing Drew's sentences with archival precision and letting Avery's slide off because Avery is not going to test me on them, and Drew, in some imagined future review, will. The calculator runs even at home. The calculator is running on the couch. The calculator decides what gets filed and what doesn't, and it has decided, without consulting me, that Avery is safe and Drew is not, and so Avery gets the leftover bandwidth.

That's the part I want to write down. The flick of irritation wasn't really about Avery saying *you know how I am.* It was about the small mortifying realization that I have been giving the most attentive version of myself to the person who has the least claim on it, and the fuzziest version to the person who is, in any sane accounting, the most important. Drew gets the storage. Avery gets the *mm.*

I don't know what to do with that exactly. I'm not going to walk in tonight and announce I'm going to start paying better attention, that would be — both weird and the kind of thing that lasts about three days. But I noticed it. I noticed who I've been filing for. I noticed which calculator is running and which isn't. The flick was small enough not to say out loud, but it was useful. It was information. The shape of the flick was *I have been somewhere else, and Avery has not yet said anything about it, and I would like to come back before Avery has to.*

Avery's in the shower. I can hear the pipes. The plant has, I swear, a third new leaf coming in. I'm going to tell Avery about all three at once, when Avery comes out. That's a small filing in the other direction. It's a start.

entries.prompt: When did you last hold back from saying something to someone — not in a fight, just in regular conversation — because saying it would have taken more energy than you had? Tell me what the unsaid thing was, and what you said or did instead.
entries.content:
Friday night, on the phone with my dad. He'd called around 7:30 which is his window — after his dinner, before whatever show he and my mom watch. I was on the couch with leftover takeout in a container next to me, the kind of dinner that is technically a dinner because it's food and you ate it.
