He was telling me about the car. The car has been a thing for two months. The car makes a noise, the mechanic said it was one thing, then it was another thing, then it was a third thing, and now my dad is in the part of the saga where he is debating whether to fix it again or sell it, and he wants to talk this through, out loud, with someone, and that someone is me. He goes through the math. He goes through the mileage. He goes through what his friend Carl said about a similar car. He goes through the resale value site he's been checking. I have, at this point, heard versions of this monologue maybe five times. I could give it myself. I could give it in his voice.

And in the middle of it, he said, "but you know me, I hate the idea of just throwing money at a problem," and there was this thing I almost said.

The thing I almost said was: Dad, you're not going to sell it. We both know you're not going to sell it. You're going to fix it again because selling it would mean admitting you should have sold it three repairs ago, and that's not a thing you do, and that's fine, but can we stop pretending this is a decision. Can we just talk about something else. Can you ask me how I am. Can you ask me anything about my actual life. I haven't told you about the spec doc. I haven't told you about Avery in any real way in months. You don't know the names of anyone I work with. You are calling me to narrate a decision you have already made and I am the audience and I love you and I am so tired.

That whole sentence, sitting right there. I could feel the shape of it in my chest. I could feel where it would start — *Dad* — and where it would end — *I'm tired.* And I knew, the way you know with parents, that if I started it I would not be able to stop it, and if I could not stop it I would say things I couldn't unsay, and then he would get quiet in the specific way he gets quiet, and then my mom would call tomorrow to ask what happened, and then I would have to manage that call too, and the whole weekend would get eaten by a thing I hadn't meant to start.

So instead I said, "yeah, that's tough, what's Carl think?"

What's Carl think. I asked about Carl. I served him the next prompt in the monologue. He took it and ran. Carl thinks fix it. Carl always thinks fix it. Carl is, my dad explained, a person who keeps cars for fifteen years on principle. And I made the noises. The Avery noises, even, the *mm, mhm,* though they felt thinner over the phone with my dad than they do with my mom, because my dad doesn't actually need them — he's not really checking whether I'm there, he's just talking — but I made them anyway because making them is easier than not.

The call went on for another twenty minutes. He covered the car, the neighbor's tree which is dropping branches into the yard, my mom's new yoga thing which he doesn't approve of but is being supportive about, and a long detour about a TV show he just discovered which is actually six years old. He asked me, near the end, "and how's work, you doing okay?" and I said "yeah, busy, good, you know," and he said "that's great honey," and we got off the phone.

I sat with the takeout container for a while.

What I did with the unsaid thing. I want to write this part carefully because I don't fully like it. I picked up my phone and I scrolled. Same as the Nat night. Same as always. I scrolled through nothing for about half an hour and then I got up and put the takeout container in the fridge and I noticed I was angry — not at my dad, not at the car, just a low free-floating angry — and I didn't know where to put it, so I texted Avery, who was at a friend's thing, just *call when you're home?* and then immediately regretted it because Avery would read that and worry, and I'd asked for something I didn't actually want which was a phone call when what I wanted was for Avery to be in the room already.

Avery wrote back *all good?* and I wrote *yeah just had a long call with my dad* and Avery wrote *oof, want me to come back?* and I wrote *no no stay, I'm fine, just venting* even though I had not vented, I had told Avery one fact and labeled it venting because labeling it venting was easier than actually venting.

This is the thing I keep doing. I hold back the thing that would take energy to say, and then I spend the same energy or more on the holding-back, and the result is I am tired and the person hasn't gotten the thing and I haven't gotten anything either. The math is bad. The calculator I keep talking about is, I am realizing, frequently terrible at math.

What I would have wanted, if I'm being honest, is for my dad to ask me one real question. *How are you, actually.* The *actually* doing the work. The same way *genuinely* did work in Drew's compliment. One adverb, one extra beat, and I would have cracked. I would have told him about the spec doc. I would have told him I'm tired. I would have told him I miss him in a way I don't know how to say on a Friday at 7:30.

He didn't ask. I didn't offer. We talked about Carl.

Avery's home now, putting keys down. I can hear it. I'm going to go say hi and not bring this up, probably, and that's its own small held-back thing. But I'm going to put my phone down first. That part I can do.

entries.prompt: Describe a recent stretch of time — an hour, an evening, a commute — where you weren't doing anything in particular and your mind kept circling back to the same thing. What was it circling, and what did you keep telling yourself about it?
entries.content:
Wednesday night, between like 9:15 and 11, on the couch, supposedly watching the show Avery picked. Avery was actually watching it. I was holding the remote like a prop and circling, for an hour and forty-five minutes, around one sentence Drew said in standup.

The sentence was: "Let's have Reese take point on the design sync going forward."

That's it. That's the whole sentence. It came at the end of standup, kind of as a logistics thing, the way Drew tosses out logistics things, and Reese said "sounds good" and I said nothing because nothing was being asked of me, I was just present at the announcement, and then we all closed the meeting and got on with our days. I had a fine day after that. Sent the thing I needed to send. Ate lunch. Did a small thing for someone else and they said thanks. The day was a normal day.

And then at 9:15 I was on the couch and the sentence came back.

*Let's have Reese take point on the design sync going forward.*

The first circle was: that used to be sort of mine. Not formally mine, nobody had ever said it was mine, but I had been the one who showed up to those design syncs the most consistently, and I had taken decent notes, and I had relayed things back to the team in slack the next day, and there was a version of the world where if Drew was assigning a point person for this it could have been me. And it wasn't. It was Reese. Drew picked Reese out loud, in front of everyone, and I was sitting there.

The second circle, like four minutes later, was: was I being moved out of something. Was this a quiet demotion. Drew didn't say *let's switch from Kai to Reese,* Drew just said *let's have Reese take point,* but the implicit thing in the sentence, when you turn it sideways, is that point used to be diffuse and now it isn't and Reese is the one holding it. Which means I'm the one not holding it. Which means at some point Drew had a thought like *Reese would be better at this* and that thought happened in Drew's head without my knowledge and I cannot now un-have it happened.

The third circle was: was the spec doc thing — the *genuinely clean* thing — a goodbye gift. Was that Drew being nice to me on the way to giving the next visible thing to Reese. Was the printer compliment a kind of severance.

I want to say, for the record, on the page, that this is insane. I know it's insane. Drew gave Reese a logistics task. Drew did not write me out of the team in a sentence. The design sync is one meeting. There are other meetings. I have not been demoted from anything because I was not promoted to anything in the first place, the design sync was a thing I happened to do, and now Reese is doing it, and that's how teams work, things move around. I know all of this. I knew it on the couch. I knew it the whole hour and forty-five minutes.

What I kept telling myself, in between the circles, was: *don't make this bigger than it is.* That was the line. That was the actual line, in those words, repeating. *Don't make this bigger than it is. Don't make this bigger than it is.* And every time I told myself that, the sentence got a little bigger, because telling yourself not to make a thing bigger is itself a way of making it bigger, you are confirming there is a thing to be sized, you are picking it up and looking at it again to check.

The other thing I kept telling myself was: *you don't even like the design sync.* Which was also true and also unhelpful. I genuinely don't like the design sync. It runs long. The design folks talk in a vocabulary I half-have. I usually leave it tired. If you'd asked me last month *would you like to be relieved of the design sync,* I would have said yes, please, take it, I have plenty of other things. So why was I sitting on the couch with my chest tight about a meeting I didn't want.

The answer, when I let myself look at it, is that I didn't want the meeting, I wanted the option of the meeting. I wanted to be the person Drew might have picked. The picking is the thing. The meeting is incidental. The meeting could have been any meeting. What I was circling, all hour and forty-five minutes, was: Drew chose Reese, in a sentence, in standup, and the choosing happened in front of me, and I now know — in a way I can't unknow — that when Drew is silently ranking us, Reese is the name that comes up first.

That's the actual thing. That's what was inside the sentence.

Avery, at one point, paused the show and said *are you watching this* and I said *yeah, sorry, just spaced out for a sec,* and Avery rewound thirty seconds and pressed play and I watched the same thirty seconds with the same level of attention which was zero, and Avery did not pause it again, which I appreciated and also which made me feel a little caught, because Avery knew I wasn't watching and was choosing not to make me admit it.

What I kept telling myself, near the end, the version of the sentence that arrived around 10:50, was: *the calculator is wrong about this.* The calculator was sitting there assigning weight to the design sync sentence — heavy, very heavy, possibly career-defining — and I kept trying to put my thumb on the scale and say *no, it's a logistics decision, it weighs almost nothing.* And the calculator and I were just disagreeing, in silence, on the couch, while Avery laughed at something on screen.

I didn't tell Avery. I'm not going to tell Avery. Or — maybe I'll tell Avery a small version tomorrow, like *Drew gave Reese the design sync and I noticed it bugged me more than it should,* which would be the practice version of the want-out-loud thing I said I'd try. Small. Specific. A little embarrassing.

Bed. Plant has the third leaf, confirmed, Avery saw it on Sunday and made a small impressed noise that I have been carrying around since.

entries.prompt: How are you talking to yourself about work lately, when no one's around to hear it? Pick a recent moment — at your desk, walking somewhere, lying awake — and tell me what the running commentary actually sounded like.
entries.content:
Last night, like 12:40, I was lying on my back staring at the ceiling and the commentary was running and I want to try to write down what it actually sounded like, because if I clean it up it loses the point.

It went something like: *okay so the design sync thing, fine, fine, you've been over this, but what's the thing after the design sync, what's the next quiet reassignment, are there things being moved off your plate that you don't even know about because nobody announces the things they don't give you, of course nobody announces it, that's the point, you only find out when Reese is suddenly running the thing, and then it's already the new normal and you can't object because objecting would mean admitting you'd been tracking it, and tracking it is the thing you're supposed to not be doing, you're supposed to be heads down doing your work, except heads down doing your work is also apparently the thing that makes you invisible, so what, what are you supposed to do, both, you're supposed to be doing both, be heads down and also be visible, be the spec doc person and also be the nine-page handoff doc person, be reliable and also be initiative, be tidy for Drew and also surprise Drew, jesus, who can do that, Reese can do that, Reese is doing that, Reese is doing it right now while you lie here, Reese is probably asleep actually, Reese sleeps fine, Reese doesn't lie awake calculating, that's why Reese is Reese and you are this.*

And then a beat, and then: *don't be dramatic.*

And then: *but am I being dramatic though.*

And then: *the spec doc was good. Drew said genuinely. Hold on to genuinely.*

And then, immediately: *one printer compliment.*

I want to write down how fast that pivot was. From *hold on to genuinely* to *one printer compliment* — half a second. The good thing arrives and the calculator is already dressing it down. *Genuinely* getting weighed against the design sync sentence, the design sync winning, the printer compliment being filed under *outdated, possibly retracted, do not lean on.*

The voice was not kind. I want to say that plainly. The voice was not the voice I would use with Avery, or with Nat, or with — anyone. If I heard someone talking to a friend the way I was talking to me at 12:40 I would think the friend should leave. The voice was clipped and impatient and a little bit sneering, in a way that had a specific cadence I want to try to catch, which is — *come on. come on. you know this. you know this.* Like a tutor who has explained the problem too many times.

*Come on. You know Drew is just managing the team. You know the design sync is admin. You know this isn't about you. Come on.*

And underneath the tutor voice, the smaller more honest voice, which only got a few words in: *but it does feel like it's about me though.*

The tutor voice did not engage with that. The tutor voice just said *come on* again and moved on.

I noticed at some point — I think it was around 12:55, because I checked the phone and immediately regretted checking the phone — that the commentary had a shape. It wasn't random. It was cycling. Design sync sentence, then Reese, then the spec doc as defense, then printer compliment as defense, then the calculator overruling both defenses, then a brief flash of *quit, just quit, find another job,* which is a thing that flares up sometimes and burns out fast because it is not a real thought, it is a flare, and then back to the design sync sentence. The cycle took maybe ninety seconds and then it started again.

I have been doing this cycle, in some form, for — a while. The objects change. Some nights it's the *kind of similar to what Reese said, basically* sentence. Some nights it's the rocket emoji I didn't get. Last night it was the design sync. The cycle is the same. It is the same shape. I am, lying on my back, walking myself through the same loop, and the loop is dressed up as thinking but it is not thinking, it is something else, it is — keeping the wound open, maybe, or checking that the wound is still there, or rehearsing the wound in case I need to defend its existence to someone later.

What was the running commentary actually saying, if I strip it down. It was saying: *you are not doing enough. you are not being seen enough. the people around you are pulling ahead. you are going to find out, eventually and embarrassingly, that you were never as good as you thought, and the moment of finding out is going to be public, and Drew is going to be there, and Reese is going to be there, and you should be preparing for it now by worrying about it.*

The commentary thinks the worrying is preparation. That's the trick. The commentary has convinced me that lying on the ceiling at 12:40 turning the design sync sentence over and over is a form of getting ready. It is not. It is just suffering with extra steps. I know this. I knew it last night. I lay there knowing it and kept doing it anyway, because the alternative — putting the sentence down, letting Drew assign Reese a meeting and not making it mean anything — felt like dropping my guard.

Around 1:10 I rolled over. Avery made a small sleep noise and shifted, and I matched my breathing to Avery's for a minute, on purpose, because I read somewhere that helps, and I don't know if it helps but it gave me something to do that wasn't the cycle. After a while I fell asleep.

This morning the design sync sentence weighed almost nothing. I made coffee, the slow way, and I thought about it once, briefly, and it was just a fact about the team, a logistics decision, a thing I could have lived with at 11am yesterday no problem. The calculator runs at a different setting in daylight. I want to remember that. The 12:40 calculator and the 7:30am calculator are not the same calculator. The 12:40 one should not be allowed to make decisions about how I feel about my career.

Going to work. Going to try to listen in standup instead of rehearse.

entries.prompt:
The last three entries have all been work-shaped — Drew, Reese, the design sync, the late-night commentary. Time to go somewhere else.

Tell me about a small kindness someone did for you recently that you almost didn't register at the time. What was it, and when did you actually let yourself feel it — if you have yet?
entries.content:
Monday morning, the bus was packed in the way it gets when it's raining and everyone who normally walks has decided no, today the bus, and I got on at my stop and there were no seats and I did the standing thing, holding the pole, with my bag between my feet so it wouldn't drip on anyone. I had headphones in but nothing was playing. I just had them in for the social barrier of it.

At the next stop a woman got on, older, with one of those collapsible carts full of groceries, and she did the thing of sort of scanning the bus for a seat and then arranging herself near the front to stand. And the guy sitting in the seat closest to her — late twenties maybe, hood up, headphones on, the whole I-am-not-here uniform — got up without making a thing of it and gestured to the seat. She said thank you and sat down. He moved to stand near me by the pole.

That's not the kindness. That part I noticed in the regular way you notice a small civil thing on a bus.

The kindness was — two stops later, the bus lurched, the way it does at that one light, and I lost my grip on the pole for a second because my hand was wet and I sort of stumbled into the guy. Not hard. Just a bump. And I said sorry the way you do, automatic, already turning my face away, and he said "you're good" and then, like a beat later, he said, "here, you can have this side, it's drier," and shifted so I had the better part of the pole, the part that wasn't slick from someone's wet jacket. And then he went back to his phone.

That was it. *Here, you can have this side, it's drier.* Eight words. From a stranger. On the bus. On a Monday.

I said thanks and held the drier part of the pole and we did not interact again for the rest of the ride. He got off two stops before mine. I never saw his face properly because of the hood. If you put me in a lineup I could not pick him out.

And the thing is, in the moment, I barely registered it. I logged it as *bus thing, fine, move on,* and went back to staring at the window and running through whatever I was running through about the day ahead — I think it was a thing about an email I needed to send, I had been drafting it in my head since I left the apartment. I noted the pole was drier and used the drier part and did not feel anything about it. The kindness happened and I took the benefit of it and kept thinking about my email.

I didn't actually feel it until last night. I was washing dishes after dinner and Avery was in the other room and my hands were in the warm water and for some reason — I have no idea why, this is the part I can't account for — the sentence came back. *Here, you can have this side, it's drier.* And I felt it. Like, a small warm thing in my chest. A guy on a bus who didn't know me moved over so my hand wouldn't be on the wet part of the pole. He didn't have to do that. Nobody would have known if he hadn't. He wasn't performing for anyone. He just — did it, and went back to his phone.

I stood there with a soapy plate and I thought about how many small things like that have probably happened to me in the last month that I didn't register. Someone holding a door. Someone letting me go first at the crosswalk. The barista at the place who knows I don't want oat milk anymore and just doesn't ask. Drew, even — Drew said *thanks for jumping on that* in slack last week and I read it as a transactional thing and moved on, but the *thanks for jumping on that* was probably also a small kindness, a thing Drew didn't have to type, and I filed it under work data and didn't let it be warm.

I think I have been running so hot on the calculator — what does it mean, what's the angle, what's the implication — that the small unweighted kindnesses just slide past. The calculator doesn't know what to do with them. They don't fit in the columns. *Stranger on bus, no agenda, eight words.* The calculator looks at that and goes *not relevant* and discards it. And then I lie awake at 12:40 wondering why I feel unwitnessed when actually I am being witnessed all day, in tiny ways, by people who are not running calculators on me at all.

Avery came into the kitchen at some point while I was still standing there with the plate and asked if I was okay and I said yeah, just spaced out, and Avery said okay and grabbed the dish towel and started drying the things in the rack. And I almost said it. I almost said *a guy on the bus this morning gave me the dry side of the pole and I just remembered it and it made me feel something.* And I didn't, because it would have sounded like a non-sequitur, and because I wanted to keep it for myself a minute longer. It felt like a thing that would shrink if I said it out loud too fast.

So I'm saying it here instead. The guy with the hood. Eight words. The drier part of the pole. I am letting myself feel it now, almost a full day late, while writing this at the kitchen table at — 9:55. Not bad. Usually it takes me longer.

The plant has four leaves. I'm going to leave the calculator in the other room when I go to bed.

entries.prompt: Think about a meal you ate recently — at home, out somewhere, at your desk, whatever. Who were you with, or were you alone, and what was the conversation in your head or across the table actually like? Tell me about the meal and what was sitting alongside it.
entries.content:
Sunday, brunch place six blocks over, the one with the green awning that Avery and I have been to maybe four times now. We went because Avery suggested it Saturday night and I said yes without doing the calculation about whether I wanted to leave the apartment, which is a small thing I am trying to do more — say yes to the going-out before the part of me that prefers the couch can build its case.

I got the eggs with the thing on top. Avery got the pancakes. We split a side of potatoes because Avery wanted them and I said I'd help. There was coffee, of course, and the coffee at this place is fine, not as good as the pour-over at home, which I am now apparently a person with opinions about. Avery noticed me make a small face after the first sip and said "the coffee at home has ruined you" and I said "the coffee at home has ruined me" and we both laughed a little and ordered orange juice.

The conversation across the table was easy. Avery told me about a friend of theirs who is maybe moving back to town, a name I had to think about for a second but did place, and I asked the right follow-up about it — *do you want them to move back* — and Avery said yes, mostly, with a small qualification about the friend being a lot in groups, and I filed that, on purpose. *Avery's friend Jamie, a lot in groups, Avery wants them back but with caveats.* I am keeping a small mental folder now. It is a thing I am doing. It has fourteen entries in it. I am aware fourteen is not very many for eight months but I am building from where I am.

We talked about the week. Avery had a hard Wednesday I'd half-tracked and I asked about it, properly, and Avery told me the longer version and I didn't fill any of the silences with *mhm* prematurely, I let Avery actually finish thoughts. Avery noticed. I could tell because at one point Avery said "you're being a good listener today" and I felt my face get warm and said "I'm trying" and Avery said "I noticed" and we moved on, and the moving on was the kindness, because if Avery had stayed on it I would have gotten self-conscious and ruined it.

The conversation in my head, alongside — okay, this is the part I want to be honest about. The conversation in my head was quieter than usual but it was still there. There was a small loop running about whether the eggs had been worth eighteen dollars. There was a smaller loop about whether I should have texted my mom back yesterday. There was a flicker, when Avery was talking about Jamie, of *Avery has more friends here than I do,* which is a true thing and a stupid thing to feel competitive about but the calculator doesn't sleep on weekends. And there was a very brief one, maybe ten seconds, about the design sync. The design sync followed me to brunch. I want to log that. The design sync sentence is, apparently, portable.

But — and this is the thing — the loops were thin. They were running in the background like a tab I'd forgotten to close, not loud. I could hear Avery over them. I think I want to remember that's possible. That the loops can be there and also not be the main thing. I have been writing about them lately like they're the whole soundtrack and they're not, always. Sometimes they're just a tab.

The food came and Avery did the thing where they reach over with a fork to try a bite of mine without asking, which used to make me weird and which I have now decided is one of my favorite things about Avery, I don't know when that flipped. I let Avery have a piece of egg. I did not, in return, take any pancake, because I don't actually like pancakes, which is a thing I had to admit out loud to Avery about three months ago and which Avery has not let me forget. Avery said "you're sure" and I said "I'm sure" and we did this little routine and it was genuinely funny in the way small repeated things between two people get funny.

What was sitting alongside the meal — that's the prompt. Sitting alongside was, I think, a feeling I want to call ordinariness, except I don't trust that word because it sounds like a downgrade. It wasn't a downgrade. It was — we were two people eating eggs. Nothing was being decided. Nothing was being performed. Avery wasn't testing me and I wasn't auditioning. The waiter was a person with a face, not a transaction. The light through the window was nice. My back didn't hurt. I had slept enough. The bill, when it came, was what I expected the bill to be.

That's it. That's the whole report. We split it, we walked home, we stopped at the corner store for milk because we were out, Avery picked the kind I like without asking. I noticed Avery doing that and didn't say anything, just held the milk on the way home like a small fact.

When we got back I sat at the table for a minute by myself while Avery went to put the milk away and I had this thought — the kind I have been having more lately, since the Saturday morning coffee thing — which was *this is what being okay feels like, again, hi.* The thought has a slightly less surprised tone now. Less *I forgot.* More *oh, here you are.* Like recognizing someone I keep running into.

I don't want to oversell this. By 4pm I was tense about an email again. By 9pm I was on my phone. The calculator came back online for the evening shift. But for the duration of brunch, and the walk home, and the milk, the calculator was off. Or low. And I want to write down that this is something I am noticing more — these patches where the volume is down, where the loops are thin, where I am just a person eating eggs across from a person eating pancakes. I want to know they are in here too. Not just the 12:40 ceiling and the design sync sentence and the printer compliment getting weighed and reweighed. The eggs too. The fork reaching over. The milk in my hand on the walk home.

Plant has four leaves still. Avery saw me looking at it just now and said "your plant" with this particular emphasis and I said "our plant" and Avery said "okay, our plant" and went into the other room. I am writing this with the dish towel still over my shoulder. I forgot to put it down.

entries.prompt: Where in your life right now are you holding two opinions at once — one you'd say out loud and one you wouldn't? Pick a recent moment when both showed up and tell me what you actually said versus what was running underneath.
entries.content:
Tuesday, in the kitchen, Avery was telling me about a thing one of their friends — Sam, the one who's been in grad school for what feels like nine years — was doing. Sam is taking a year off the program. Just stopping. Going to do some kind of fellowship thing in another city, maybe come back, maybe not. Avery was telling me this in an admiring way. *Sam's just doing it. Sam's not waiting.* Avery thinks Sam is brave.

What I said, out loud, leaning against the counter: "yeah, that's cool, good for Sam, I think it's smart to follow that when you have it."

What was running underneath: are you fucking kidding me. Sam is thirty-one and has been in school for almost a decade and is now taking *another* year to do a fellowship that sounds like a fancy way to be unemployed, and the people around Sam are calling that brave, and meanwhile I have been at my job for eight months losing sleep over whether I am performing well enough for a manager I see four hours a week, and nobody is going to call me brave for any of it, and I cannot tell if I am jealous of Sam or contemptuous of Sam, and I think the honest answer is both at once, and the both-at-once is the part I cannot say at the kitchen counter to my partner who clearly likes Sam.

That was the whole sentence. *Both at once.* I was jealous of Sam because Sam apparently has the kind of life where you can just — pause. Stop the thing. Try the other thing. Sam has, presumably, savings or parents or a partner or a tolerance for risk, or all four, and I have none of those four in the configuration that would let me pause anything. And I was contemptuous of Sam because — and this is the ugly part — I think the calculator in me has decided that what Sam is doing is the thing people do when they don't have to be good at one thing for long enough to find out if they are. Sam gets to keep being potential. Sam gets to keep being *about to.* I have been envying *about to* my whole life and the job is the first thing that has made me actually be in something instead of about to be in something, and now I'm watching Sam go back to about-to and Avery is calling it brave.

I said *good for Sam, I think it's smart to follow that when you have it.* Which is — a sentence I don't fully believe. The *when you have it* was doing a lot of work in that sentence. *When you have it* was me sneaking the qualification in. *When you have it* meant: Sam has it because Sam has whatever makes it possible, and most people don't have that, and so the bravery story is a little bit of a flattering frame. But I said it small enough that it slid past as agreement.

Avery said something like "yeah, I think it's the right call for them," and we moved on to whether we needed onions.

What I wouldn't have said out loud, on top of the both-at-once thing: I am also a little hurt that you think Sam is brave. I think I want you to think *I'm* brave. I don't do anything brave. I do my list. I send the email. I water the plant. The bravery in my life looks like signing up for a gym I haven't gone to. So when you say Sam is brave I hear, in a tiny ungenerous register, *and you, by contrast, are not.* Which is an insane thing to put on Avery, who was just talking about a friend, who said nothing at all about me, who is allowed to have admirations that are not about me. But the calculator runs even on sentences that are not addressed to me. The calculator hears *brave* and immediately measures itself against *brave* and finds itself wanting and then resents the source of the word.

I didn't say any of that. I said *yeah, that's cool.* I cut the onions. I made a face Avery didn't see, brief, just a little flicker of *come on,* and then I was past it and we were making dinner.

The thing I keep noticing — and this is, I think, what the prompt is actually asking — is that the two opinions aren't really an opinion and a hidden contradicting opinion. It's more like the surface opinion is the one I think a generous person would have, and the underneath opinion is the one I actually have, and I serve up the generous one because I would like to be a generous person, and the underneath one stays in the kitchen with me. *Good for Sam* is the version I am working toward. *Are you fucking kidding me* is the version I currently am. And the gap between them is not nothing. The gap is most of who I am at 24, probably. Performing toward a version of me that is more generous than the version of me that's typing.

I think this is also what was happening with Reese, all along. The *good for Reese, the doc is good* is the surface. The *Reese is a try-hard* is the underneath. I have written about this. I know this. What I hadn't quite seen was that this is a structure, not a Reese-specific event. I do this with everyone whose existence pricks at me. Sam now. Reese still. The college LinkedIn person on the bus. There's the said thing and the underneath thing and I keep a careful seal between them because if the seal cracks I think I will be revealed as small.

Maybe the seal is fine. Maybe everyone has the seal. Maybe Avery has a seal too and what Avery says about Sam is also the surface version and underneath Avery has some other more complicated feeling about a friend who keeps not finishing things. I don't know. I haven't asked. I won't ask, because asking would imply I know about seals, and knowing about seals is its own kind of admission.

We had pasta. It was fine. Avery put too much salt in, which Avery does, and I didn't say anything about it, which is its own small held-back opinion, but a generous one, I think. I'm allowed some of those.

Plant: four leaves, holding steady. The fifth one I thought I saw last week was just the angle.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel genuinely proud of yourself for something small — something nobody else would have clocked as an accomplishment? Tell me what it was and whether you let yourself sit with the proud feeling or moved past it quickly.
entries.content:
Thursday, the bus, going home. There was a guy across the aisle eating something with a strong smell — not bad, just strong, some kind of takeout container with a sauce — and the bus was crowded and warm and a woman two rows up was on speakerphone with her speaker pointed at the rest of us, and normally — I want to be specific about what normally means — normally by the second stop I would have my jaw clenched and a small mean monologue running about how this city does not respect basic public-space norms, and by the fourth stop I would be performing exasperation in my own head at an audience of nobody, and by my stop I would get off the bus already half-tense, carrying the bus into the apartment with me.

That didn't happen. I just — sat there. I noticed the smell. I noticed the speakerphone. I had the small flare of *come on* and then I just let it pass through. I looked out the window. The light was doing the late-afternoon thing on the buildings. I thought about what I wanted for dinner. The speakerphone lady got off at the next stop and I watched her go and didn't even feel relieved, because I hadn't been holding it. I had been on the bus.

That's it. That's the proud thing. I rode a bus without making the bus into a story.

I know how that sounds. I am aware that *did not get internally annoyed at strangers* is not, by any normal accounting, an accomplishment. Nobody clocks it. There is no one to clock it. Avery did not greet me at the door with *and how was your tolerance of public nuisance today.* Drew will not put it on a review. The bus driver did not notice. The woman with the phone did not notice, because she was on her phone. The proud feeling has, in the entire material world, an audience of one, which is me, sitting at the kitchen table at 9:30 writing this down.

But I want to say why it counted. The reason it counted is that I have been, for months, the kind of person who turns small frictions into evidence. Bus is loud, therefore the city is wrong, therefore my life is hard, therefore I am justified in being brittle when I get home. The chain is fast and I almost never see it happen. I just arrive home brittle and assume that's the weather. And on Thursday — for whatever reason, I can't even tell you why, I had not slept especially well, work had been normal, nothing in particular had gone right that day — the chain didn't fire. The smell was a smell. The phone was a phone. The bus was a bus. I got home regular.

I noticed it as I was unlocking the door. I had this little flicker of *huh, I'm not in a mood,* and then I noticed I noticed, and then I had the small proud thing.

Did I sit with it. I want to be honest. I sat with it for maybe forty seconds. I stood in the entryway with my keys in my hand and I let the proud feeling be a feeling. It was a quiet one. It wasn't the *enormous* of the printer compliment. It wasn't even the warm of the bus pole guy. It was more like — a small okay. *You did a thing you usually don't do, and the thing was not doing a thing.* The proud was for the absence of a small ugliness. Which is a strange shape for proud to have but I think it's the right shape for this one.

And then I moved past it, the way I knew I would. I went into the kitchen. Avery was reheating soup and asked how my day was and I said *fine, good, normal,* and didn't tell Avery about the bus. I don't think I could have told Avery about the bus without making it weird. *I rode a bus without getting annoyed at strangers and I'm proud of myself* — I tried that sentence in my head and it did not survive contact. It would have required so much explaining. The whole chain. The brittle weather. The way I usually am. To explain why it was an accomplishment I'd have to first explain that I am normally a person small enough that not being small is news, and I didn't want to do that, not in the kitchen, not on a Thursday.

So I kept it. I kept the forty seconds in the entryway and I let the rest of the night be regular. Avery and I had soup. We watched some of the show. I went to bed at a reasonable hour. The proud thing did not get bigger and did not get smaller. It just sat where I left it.

What I'm noticing, writing this — and I want to be careful because I have a tendency to convert everything into a recipe — is that the small private prouds might be the only kind I actually get to keep. The big ones, the ones with audiences, those get processed by the calculator immediately. *Genuinely* gets weighed against the rocket emoji. The eggs across from Avery get an asterisk for *yes but you're tense by 4.* The calculator can metabolize almost any visible good thing into a draw or a loss. But the bus thing — the calculator doesn't know what to do with the bus thing. There is no column for *did not get internally annoyed.* There is no metric. The calculator looks at it and shrugs, and the proud feeling, unweighed, just stays there.

I don't think I'm going to start collecting these. That's also the calculator talking — *make it a system, make it a practice, turn the noticing into a thing you do.* No. The bus thing was the bus thing. If I try to make it the start of something I'll ruin it by Tuesday.

I'm just going to log it here. I rode a bus and didn't make a story out of it. Forty seconds in the entryway. Soup with Avery. Bed.

Plant has four leaves. The same four. Still fine.

entries.prompt: Last weekend you said yes to brunch before the couch-preferring side of you could build its case. When did you most recently lose that small negotiation — pick a specific evening or afternoon — and what did the talking-yourself-out-of-it actually sound like?
entries.content:
Friday night. Ben was going to be in town. Ben — Ben, who I have not seen in fourteen months, who texted the group chat on Tuesday saying *I'm gonna be in your city Fri-Sun for a wedding thing, drinks Friday whoever's around,* and Nat replied with the wrong-city joke and Priya replied with three exclamation points and a heart, and I replied *omg yes, in,* and Ben sent me the address of the bar separately and said *7pm-ish, come whenever, I'll be the tired one in the corner,* and I said *can't wait.*

I can't wait. I typed *can't wait* with two exclamation points. On Tuesday I meant it.

By Friday at 6:15 I was on the couch in the soft pants and the case was already half-built. The case sounded like this:

*You're tired. You had a long week. The conference room thing on Wednesday wiped you. You don't have it in you to be on tonight. Ben is going to want stories and you're going to have to perform stories and the stories you have are all work and Ben doesn't care about work, so you'll have to either be boring or invent a more interesting version of yourself for two hours, and either way you'll come home depleted.*

That was the first wave. The second wave was logistical. *The bar is forty minutes on the bus. Forty there, forty back. That's eighty minutes of bus on a Friday. You'll get home at midnight at the earliest. You won't sleep til one. Saturday will be wrecked.*

The third wave was the meanest one. *Ben isn't going to mind. Ben said come whenever. Ben said I'll be the tired one in the corner. That's not a real invitation, that's a casual one. Nat isn't going. Priya isn't going. It's just going to be you, alone, in a bar, with a person you haven't seen in over a year, doing the catch-up. And the catch-up is going to make the not-having-caught-up obvious. Ben is going to ask about Avery and you're going to realize you've barely told Ben anything about Avery. Ben is going to ask about work and you're going to give the elevator pitch and Ben is going to nod and then there'll be the silence where you're supposed to ask Ben something and you'll have to fish for what to ask because you don't actually know what Ben's been up to either, you've been letting the chat coast, and Ben will know, and you'll know Ben knows, and the whole evening will be a mutual quiet acknowledgment that we used to be close and now we are this.*

That last wave is the one that did it. The bus and the tired and the pants — those I could have overruled. The *we used to be close and now we are this* I could not.

At 6:40 I texted Ben. *hey — so sorry, I'm wiped from this week, can we do brunch tomorrow instead? my treat.* Sent. The *my treat* was the guilt-tax I added to make the cancellation sound like I valued the seeing. I noticed myself adding it. I sent it anyway.

Ben wrote back at 6:52. *no worries!! brunch sounds great. text me in the am.*

The double exclamation points were generous. The double exclamation points were Ben being a friend about it. And I read them and I felt the relief — the same Tuesday-night-Nat relief, the *oh thank god* — and then immediately, on its heels, the second thing, which is the thing I am apparently going to keep having until I stop doing this: *Ben let me off the hook and now I owe Ben more than I would have owed Ben if I'd just gone.*

I sat there with the phone. I had the whole evening back. Eighty minutes of bus, three hours at the bar, eighty back — five hours, I'd just bought myself five hours, and I knew, I *knew* the way you know with a hangover coming on, that I was about to do exactly what I did the Nat night. Scroll. Sink. Convert the saved time into nothing.

I tried to head it off. I said out loud, to nobody, *okay, do something with this.* Avery was at the gym — Avery actually goes to a gym, the irony is not lost — and I had the apartment to myself and I had the option, theoretically, of doing any number of things. I could have read. I could have called my mom back. I could have written. I could have, god, gone to the gym myself, the membership is right there, six minutes away.

I scrolled for an hour and forty minutes.

Then I ordered food. Then I ate the food on the couch. Then I scrolled some more. Then Avery got home around 9:30 and asked how Ben was and I said *we moved it to brunch tomorrow, I was just too fried* and Avery said *makes sense* in the voice that I cannot read, the voice that might be neutral and might be the smaller version of *okay.* And I felt, briefly, found out, even though Avery didn't push and I have no evidence Avery was pushing.

I want to write down what the talking-myself-out-of-it actually sounded like underneath all the surface waves, because I think the surface ones — tired, bus, logistics — were cover. The actual sentence underneath, if I let it surface, was: *if you go you will find out the friendship has shrunk, and you will have to feel that in real time, in a bar, with your face doing things. If you cancel you can keep the friendship the size it used to be in your head a little longer.*

The case the couch-preferring side of me built was, in the end, a case for not finding out. The cancellation wasn't really about energy. It was about preservation. I traded one evening of possibly-awkward-actual-Ben for an indefinite continuation of remembered-Ben, who is easier to carry, who fits in the chat, who doesn't require me to know what I've missed.

Brunch is in nine hours. I am going to go. I am going to go and I am going to find out what I'm going to find out, and Ben is going to be Ben and I am going to be whoever I am at 24 across a table from a person who knew me at 19, and the gap will be whatever size it is, and I will not get to negotiate it down by skipping. I already skipped the easy version. Brunch is the version I can't skip without it meaning something.

Setting an alarm. Not scrolling. Plant has four leaves.

entries.prompt: There's a particular feeling that comes up when you watch someone in your life get praised for something you'd never get praised for — or wouldn't let yourself want praise for. When did that show up most recently, and what did you do with it?
entries.content:
Sunday, the brunch I actually went to with Ben — I want to write about that, but not the part you'd think. The part that's been sitting with me is something Ben said about Priya.

We were maybe forty minutes in. The catch-up had gone better than I'd feared and worse than I'd hoped, which is, I'm realizing, just what catch-up is. Ben asked about Avery and I had real answers. Ben asked about work and I gave the short version and didn't oversell it. Then Ben said, "did you see what Priya posted last week?" and I said no, because the chat has been thin and I haven't been clicking through, and Ben pulled out the phone and showed me.

It was Priya at some kind of community thing she'd organized in her city. A volunteer event for a thing I am not going to describe because I'd butcher it. She'd put it together, apparently, mostly herself. Ben had been there the previous time she'd done one. There was a photo of Priya looking tired and happy with a clipboard, and a caption that was not bragging, the way Priya is not a bragger, just naming the people who'd shown up to help. Ben said, "she's incredible. honestly. like she just does this stuff. she's been doing it since college and she just keeps doing it."

She's incredible. Honestly.

I felt it. The flick. Smaller than the Reese flick, different shape, but the same family. *She's incredible.* Ben said it about Priya the way Drew might say *genuinely* about a doc. Adverb doing real work. And the flick was — I don't even want to type this, but the seal cracked a little at the table, so I might as well — the flick was, *nobody is going to say "she's incredible, honestly" about me.* Not for what I do. Drew might say *clean.* Drew might say *solid.* Avery might say *good listener.* Nobody is going to say *incredible.*

And the deeper flick, the one I tried to stuff back down before it got to my face: I would not let myself want what Priya has. The thing Ben was praising her for — running a community thing, on her own time, for nothing, for years — is a thing I have never once tried to do, and would not try to do, because I cannot picture myself doing it without the calculator running underneath asking what it was for. I would want it to count toward something. I would want someone to clock it. The fact that Priya does it and apparently does not need it to count is part of why she gets the *incredible.* And I cannot get the *incredible* because I cannot do the not-counting kind of thing. So I am locked out of that praise by the shape of myself.

That's not jealousy exactly. Jealousy would be wanting Priya's life. I don't want Priya's life. I would not run a community thing if you paid me. The feeling is more like — watching a kind of praise exist that I am structurally ineligible for, and feeling the smallness of the bin I am eligible for by comparison. *Clean. Solid. Reliable.* Adjectives for an appliance.

What I did with it, in the moment, across from Ben — I made the right face. I said, "that's so Priya, that's amazing." I meant it about Priya. I also meant the *that's so Priya* in a way that quietly returned her to a category, the category of *people who do that kind of thing,* which is a category I am not in and which I was, a little bit, fencing off so I wouldn't have to ask why I'm not in it. *That's so Priya* lets Priya be the kind of person who does that and lets me be the kind of person who admires it from across a table. Roles assigned, no further questions.

Ben put the phone away and we moved on. Talked about Ben's job, which is fine, which is normal, which is the kind of job I have too. Talked about whether Nat is okay, because Nat has been weird in the chat lately, and we both noticed and neither of us has said anything to Nat. We sat with that for a second, the both of us not having said anything, and Ben said "I should text Nat" and I said "yeah, me too," and neither of us, I bet, will today.

On the bus home — and I rode the bus home from brunch the same way I rode it home from work on the *did not get annoyed* day, just sitting, watching the light — I let myself stay with it a little longer. The Priya thing. The *incredible* thing. What I noticed is that the part of me that wants to be incredible is the same part that wants the thrilled-to-announce post, the same part that wants Drew's rocket emoji, the same part that closed the promotion video fast. It is the part that wants praise to be visible and significant and from a source that confers status. Priya's praise is visible and significant but the source — a community thing nobody at my job has heard of — confers nothing my calculator recognizes. So my calculator looks at *Priya is incredible* and says *not relevant for our purposes,* and I feel the flick anyway, because the flick is older than the calculator. The flick is just *someone got called something good and you didn't.*

I don't know what to do with this. I'm not going to start a community thing. I am not going to engineer a way to get called incredible. Probably what I am going to do is keep doing my list, and keep being clean and solid, and let Priya be incredible, and let the flick be a flick. But I want to log that the flick has a shape now. It is not just *I want praise.* It is *I want praise of a kind I have decided I cannot earn, from sources I would not respect for giving it, and I resent the people who get praise of a kind I have decided I do not want.* Which is, when you write it out, an extremely tangled thing to be carrying around at brunch.

Plant: still four. Texting Nat tonight. Small. Just hi.

entries.prompt: When did you last find yourself stretching the truth a little — not a real lie, just shaping a story or an answer to land a certain way? Walk me through the moment, who you were talking to, and what you were adjusting for.
entries.content:
Wednesday, in the kitchen at work, the little one with the bad coffee that nobody actually drinks, I was filling my water bottle and a guy from another team — I'll call him the guy from another team, because I genuinely can't remember his name even though we've talked four times, which is its own thing — said hi and then said, "how's that spec stuff going, didn't you wrap something big recently?"

I said, "yeah, the design folks are using it as kind of a template now."

That's what I said. *Kind of a template.*

What Drew actually said, by the printer, four weeks ago, was *I forwarded it to the design folks as an example of what I want these to look like.* An example. Singular. One forward, one email, presumably opened by some number of people, possibly read by fewer. *An example* and *kind of a template* are not the same sentence. *An example* is a thing Drew did once. *Kind of a template* implies a process, an ongoing reference, a small institution. *Kind of a template* implies that other people, plural, are looking at my doc and structuring their docs around it.

I don't know that to be true. I have no evidence that's true. It might be true. It might be that design has, in fact, started using it as a reference and Drew just hasn't told me. But I don't know. What I know is one sentence, by a printer, four weeks ago. And I converted it, in real time, in front of a guy whose name I don't remember, into *kind of a template.*

The guy said, "oh nice, that's a big deal." And I said, "yeah, it was cool," and screwed the cap on my water bottle, and we did the small nod, and I left.

I want to write down what I was adjusting for, because the adjustment was so fast I almost didn't catch it. The guy had asked because he'd heard something. He'd heard *something,* which means somebody had said something to somebody, which means there is a small piece of social information about me circulating that is *Kai did a good doc.* And I was, in the kitchen, presented with the chance to either confirm-and-resize or confirm-and-amplify. *Yeah, Drew said it was clean* would have been confirm-and-resize. Accurate. Modest. Probably underselling it slightly, in fact, because *genuinely clean* was the actual sentence and I would have shaved the *genuinely* off out of habit. But within the truth.

I picked confirm-and-amplify. *Kind of a template.* And I picked it because — and this is the part — I have been so tired of being clean and solid and reliable that when somebody handed me an opening to be a person whose work has *traction,* I took it. Traction is the word I think I was reaching for. Reese's nine-page doc has traction. People reference it. People use it. *Kind of a template* was me, in the kitchen, trying to put my doc in the same conversation as Reese's doc, even though nobody had brought up Reese, even though the guy from another team is not on our team and probably doesn't know about Reese's doc at all. The comparison was happening only inside me, and I dressed up my answer to win an argument the guy didn't know I was having.

It was such a small stretch. Two words, *kind of.* The hedge inside the stretch — *kind of* — was even doing the work of making the stretch deniable. *I didn't say it was a template, I said kind of a template.* If anyone ever called me on it I'd have an out. *Kind of* is the politer cousin of *basically.* Same family. Same job. A structural beam holding up a sentence with no other support.

I noticed it on the way back to my desk. I had this little flicker of — *that wasn't quite right.* And then I thought, who would even know. The guy doesn't know Drew. Drew is not going to hear about it. The chain of accountability for the sentence *kind of a template* runs literally nowhere. I could let it stand. I could let the guy from another team carry away the impression that I am a person whose docs become templates, and the impression would do whatever impressions do — pass through one or two more conversations, maybe, and then dissolve, harmless.

Except I'd carry it. I'd know I'd said it. And every time I saw the guy from another team, for the next however long, there'd be this small live wire of *he thinks the doc is a template and you let him think that.* It would not be a big wire. It would be a small one. But it would be in the wall.

Sat at my desk and thought about it for maybe ten minutes. Couldn't really focus. Considered, briefly, slacking the guy something corrective, like *hey, just to be clear, Drew sent it to design as one example, not exactly a template, didn't mean to oversell it lol.* Drafted that message in my head and immediately knew I wouldn't send it, because sending it would draw attention to the stretch, which had been small enough that the guy had probably already forgotten it, and the correction would be weirder than the original. The correction would also itself be a kind of performance — *look how scrupulous I am, look how careful with the truth.* Which is its own stretch in the other direction.

So I didn't send it. I just sat with the small wire in the wall.

The thing I want to log is — I do this more than I think I do. I shape answers. *Busy* into *swamped.* *Drew said it was clean* into *Drew uses my doc as a reference.* *I'm pushing on a thing* into *I'm leading on a thing.* Tiny upgrades, all of them deniable, all of them in service of presenting a slightly more impressive version of myself to people whose impression of me, on examination, I have no reason to be optimizing this hard. The guy from another team. Why am I optimizing for the guy from another team. He doesn't know my name either, probably. He's been calling me Kai when he means Kai but I bet if you asked him to identify me on my team he'd point and go *that one.*

Avery asked at dinner what was new at work and I said *nothing much, normal week,* which is also a small adjustment, in the other direction this time. Down instead of up. Because telling Avery about the kitchen exchange would have meant explaining the stretch, and explaining the stretch would have meant admitting I am a person who stretches in kitchens with strangers about my own work, and I wasn't ready, on a Wednesday, to be that legible.

Plant: four leaves. Going to bed earlier than usual. Not going to draft any more corrective slacks in my head.

entries.prompt: Tell me about something you've been carrying around physically lately — a jacket you keep wearing, a bag, a notebook, a thing in your pocket, headphones, whatever. Why that one, and what's it doing for you?
entries.content:
The notebook. The small one, the navy one, the one with the elastic band that I have been pretending isn't slowly losing its tension. It lives in my bag. It has lived in my bag since — I want to say March, I think March, when I bought it at the place near the office on a lunch break for reasons I did not at the time articulate. I told myself I needed *a place for meeting notes.* That was the framing. *A place for meeting notes.*

I have not, in seven months, used it for meeting notes.

What's in it is — okay. The first ten pages are, technically, attempts at meeting notes. They peter out around the third week. After that it is something else. Phrases I want to remember. Words Drew has used about other people's work, written down so I can study what Drew gives praise for. *Tight.* *Clear.* *Right altitude.* That last one Drew said about somebody's doc and I wrote it down on the bus with a pen I don't even remember owning. *Right altitude.* I have looked at that page like nine times trying to decode what altitude my own writing is at.

There are also lists. Not to-do lists. Lists of — sentences I might say in 1:1s. Lists of *things I have done this quarter,* as if I am preparing for an evaluation nobody has scheduled. There is a page that just says *what is the thing nobody is doing that I could do* with three bullet points underneath, all of them crossed out. There's a page where I drafted, in pen, the slack message I would send to Drew if I were brave enough to say *am I tracking,* and then on the next page I drafted three slightly less brave versions, each one more tepid, until the final draft was basically *quick check-in?* which is the version I never sent either.

I carry it everywhere. I mean everywhere. The bag goes to work, the bag goes to brunch, the bag goes on the bus, and the notebook is in the bag, and I have not, like, taken it out at brunch — that would be weird, that would be the kind of thing where Avery would say *what is that* and I would have to explain — but it has been in the bag *at* brunch, and that counts for something, I think, in my own internal accounting. The notebook is in the room.

What I write in it is not, mostly, useful. I want to be honest about that. I write in it on the bus. I write in it at my desk during meetings I should be paying more attention to. I write in it, sometimes, at the kitchen table after Avery has gone to bed, when this — the journal — would be the more honest place to put the thing, and I put it in the notebook instead because the notebook is more — guarded, somehow. The journal is the place I have to look at things directly. The notebook is the place I can pretend I'm strategizing.

And that's the thing. That's what the notebook is doing for me. It is letting me pretend the calculator is a project. The calculator runs all day anyway — the *what does Drew mean, how does Reese do it, what's the next visible thing* — and the notebook is where I let it deposit its outputs in handwriting, which feels productive, because writing it down by hand is a thing serious people do. The notebook turns the loop into a practice. It is the loop wearing a tie.

I had this realization on the bus on Thursday. I had the notebook open on my lap and I was writing — I'm not even going to dress this up — I was writing down phrases I had heard Reese use in standup. *Spinning up.* *Loop you in.* *Quick gut check.* Reese-isms. I was cataloging Reese-isms, with a pen, on the bus, like a person studying a foreign language they hope to be mistaken for a native speaker of. And I closed the notebook mid-page because I had this small wave of — *what are you doing.* What are you doing. Are you going to start saying *spinning up* in standup. Yes, probably. Will it work. Maybe. Will it feel like yours. No.

I almost left the notebook on the bus. I had a real flicker of *just leave it, just let it go, get up at your stop and leave it on the seat and let some stranger find it and try to figure out who this person is.* I didn't. I put it back in the bag. I am not the kind of person who leaves a notebook on a bus. Even my self-sabotage has limits.

What it's doing for me, charitably — because I have been writing about this notebook like it's only a problem and that's not quite right either — is that it is an external place for the calculator's outputs. The calculator runs whether I have the notebook or not. With the notebook, at least some of the running ends up on a page, which means some of it is out of my head. The 12:40 ceiling cycles would probably be worse without it. The notebook absorbs some of the ambient anxiety the way the plant, weirdly, absorbs some of the apartment's loneliness — by being there, by being a thing I tend to.

But it is also a thing I am tending to instead of tending to other things. The hours I have spent with the notebook open on my lap are hours I have not spent reading, calling Nat back without prompting, going to the gym I am paying for, looking at Avery. The notebook is where I put attention I am not putting elsewhere. It is, in that sense, a small mirror for the calculator itself.

The elastic is going. I noticed yesterday. It's looser than it was. Eventually it will stop holding the cover closed and the notebook will splay open in the bag and pages will get bent. I will, when that happens, probably go buy another one. I have already, at some level, picked out the next one. I saw it at the same place. Slightly bigger, dark green. I am pretending I haven't decided.

Maybe I won't. Maybe when this one's elastic dies I'll just — not replace it. Carry the bag without the notebook for a week. See what that's like. See what the calculator does when it doesn't have its little office to deposit things in. I am writing that down here and I do not, fully, believe I'll do it. But I have written it down, in the journal, not in the notebook, which is something.

Plant: four leaves. The notebook is on the table next to me. I haven't opened it tonight. Small win.

entries.prompt: Last week, when someone asked you a question and you gave an answer that was a little more polished than the truth, what did the polished version sound like? Tell me about a recent moment — who asked, what you said, and what the rougher version would've been.
entries.content:
Tuesday, my mom called. The Tuesday call, not the Sunday call, which means something is up — Tuesday calls are her bonus calls, the ones she makes when she has been thinking about me in a way that won't keep until Sunday. I picked up because I was on the bus and the alternative was looking at my phone pretending to do something else.

She asked, "honey, how are you really doing, you sounded tired last weekend."

*Really.* The adverb. Same family as Drew's *genuinely.* One extra beat that is supposed to crack me open.

What I said was: "I'm good, mom, honest. Work has been busy but in a good way. I've been getting more responsibility, which is, you know, what I wanted. Avery and I are good. I've been trying to take better care of myself — I joined a gym, actually."

I joined a gym actually.

Let me itemize.

*Busy in a good way.* Busy is true. *In a good way* is — a frame. Busy has been in the way of sleep, in the way of friendships, in the way of being a person whose chest is not tight at 12:40am. The *good way* I attached to it was the version of busy I would put in a holiday letter. The version of busy that ladders up to something.

*More responsibility.* I cannot tell you what additional responsibility I have been given. I wrote about Drew giving Reese the design sync. That is the opposite of being given more responsibility. That is, in the privacy of my own ceiling, being given less. What I had in mind when I said *more responsibility* was nothing specific. It was vibes. It was the spec doc and the printer compliment and a vague sense that I am, in some way I cannot name, growing. *More responsibility* sounds like the kind of thing someone says when their career is on a track. Saying it makes me feel like I am on a track. The saying is the track.

*Avery and I are good.* This one is true, mostly. We are good. We are also the people who had the *you know how I am about that* moment that made me realize I have not been filing Avery as carefully as I file Drew. We are the people who sat through the hard week without me telling Avery the actual texture of the hard week. *Good* is accurate and also doing a lot of erasure. The rougher version would have been *we are good, and I am the worse one, and Avery has been generous about it, and I am working on it slowly.* But that is not a thing you say to your mother on a bus.

*I joined a gym, actually.* I want to be honest about this one. I have not been to the gym. I have a membership. The membership is six weeks old. I have been to the gym zero times. The gym is a charge on my credit card statement, not a building I have entered. *I joined a gym* is, technically, true. *I joined a gym, actually* — with the *actually,* with the little upward punctuation that suggests follow-up — implies that I am a person who goes. I let it imply.

My mom said, "oh honey, that's wonderful, I'm so proud of you, your dad will be so glad to hear that."

So proud.

I felt the warm thing for about three seconds and then I felt sick. Because I had — I had paid for the *so proud* with a sentence I cannot back up. My mom is going to tell my dad. My dad is going to mention it on the next call. *How's the gym going.* And I will have to either lie again or come clean, and the come-clean version, by then, will have grown teeth, because the original lie will have sat in their heads for a week becoming a thing.

The rougher version. Okay. The rougher version, if I had been brave on the bus, would have been: *mom, I am tired in a way that is not about sleep. I keep one person's opinion of me too high in my head and I cannot put it down. There's someone at work who is better at being visible than I am and I am quietly losing to her in my own mind every day. I joined a gym and I have not gone once and I am embarrassed about it and the embarrassment is part of why I haven't gone, which is the kind of loop I am stuck in. Avery is a good thing. Avery is maybe the best thing. I have been giving Avery the leftover version of my attention and I know it. I am okay. I am okay in the way you are okay when you are 24 and nothing is actually broken but you are also not loose anywhere. I am tight in every place a person can be tight.*

That's like nine sentences and not one of them is sayable to my mother on a bus on a Tuesday at 6:10pm.

What I would have settled for, the achievable rougher version: *honestly mom, work is hard in a way I'm still figuring out, and I joined a gym but I haven't actually gone yet, isn't that funny.* Two clauses. A small admission. *Isn't that funny* would have done the softening work and given her something to be warm about without me having to pretend the gym was already a thing.

I didn't say it. I said *I joined a gym, actually.* My mom said *so proud.* I said *thanks mom, I should go, the bus is almost at my stop,* which was true, and we got off and I sat on the bus for two more stops past mine because I needed to not go inside yet.

What I am noticing, writing this — the polished version was not crafted, it was reflexive. I did not pause and choose the upgrade. The upgrade was the answer. The polished version is the version that comes out when she asks *how are you really.* Which means, even with the *really* doing its work, even with my mother specifically inviting the rougher version, what surfaces is the version with the gym. The polish is faster than the truth. The polish gets there first.

I should go to the gym this week. Just so the lie becomes retroactive truth. But that is — that is the calculator running again. *Make the lie true so the lie isn't a lie.* That is not why a person goes to the gym.

I'll go because I said I would. Or I won't, and I'll have to say so eventually. Either way the sentence is out there now, sitting in my mom's head, with a little glow on it I put there. Plant: four leaves. Notebook closed on the table.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel genuinely at ease around someone — no calculating, no shaping the answer, no checking how you were landing? Tell me where you were, who you were with, and what the two of you were actually talking about.
entries.content:
Saturday afternoon, the laundromat on the corner. I want to say upfront that I did not expect the laundromat to be the answer to this prompt. I expected it to be Avery, or Avery and the eggs, or one of the recent Avery moments, because Avery is the obvious answer. But Avery is not the answer this time. The answer is Mira, who runs the laundromat, who I have apparently been having a relationship with for like six months without quite registering it as a relationship.

Avery and I have a washer in the building but the dryer is broken again, so I'd hauled the wet stuff over in the canvas bag with the broken zipper, and Mira was at the counter doing a sudoku and looked up and went "you again, with the bag," in the voice of someone who has been making a small running joke with me about the bag for months. I said "me again with the bag," and she said "you ever going to fix that zipper," and I said "no, the zipper is part of the bag's personality at this point," and she made a sound that was halfway between a laugh and a sigh and went back to her sudoku.

I loaded the dryer and sat down in one of the plastic chairs, and Mira from behind the counter said, without looking up, "the middle one eats quarters, use the one on the end," which was information she had given me before but which I appreciated again because I had been about to use the middle one. I said thanks. She said mhm. I sat there.

Here's the thing. I had my phone in my pocket and I did not take it out. I had the notebook in the bag and I did not take it out. I just sat in the plastic chair and watched my dryer turn and Mira do her sudoku and a cat I had not previously known about wander out from somewhere behind the counter and sit on a folded towel like the towel had been put there for the cat, which maybe it had.

After a while Mira said, "the cat's name is Beans," without prompting, and I said "Beans is a great name," and she said "Beans is an idiot," in the affectionate voice of someone who has lived with an idiot for a long time, and I laughed and she half-smiled at her sudoku and that was the whole exchange about Beans.

Some time later — I don't know, fifteen minutes? twenty? — she asked if I wanted the radio on and I said sure, and she put on the radio, which was playing the kind of station that plays one song from 1987 and then one song from 2003 with no governing logic, and we listened to it. We did not talk through the songs. We just sat in the laundromat with the radio and the dryer and Beans.

At some point she asked me, in a normal voice, "you the one with the partner who does the marathon thing?" and I said "no, that's not us, Avery does the gym but not — no marathons," and she said "huh, must be the other one," and that was the entire confusion, and it dissolved without either of us having to perform around it. She had me confused with someone else and we both just noted it and moved on.

What I am noticing, writing this, is that the calculator did not run once. Not once. Mira was not someone I was performing for. Mira is also not someone I was coasting with the way I sometimes coast with Avery, the half-attention thing. I was just there. I was a person at a laundromat and she was a person running a laundromat and we had a small running joke about a bag and there was a cat. There was no version of me to maintain. I was not being the work voice. I was not being the partner voice. I was not being the daughter voice. I was just the bag person, which apparently is a voice I can be without rehearsing.

We talked, at one point, about the building across the street, which is being renovated forever. She had opinions. I had vaguer opinions that solidified slightly in response to hers. We agreed the scaffolding had been up too long. We disagreed mildly about whether the new awnings on the bakery next door were an improvement. She thought yes. I thought they looked corporate. She said "corporate, you sound like my son," and that was — that was the part where I noticed I had been at ease for I don't know how long, because the *you sound like my son* landed in me as a small warm thing instead of as data to process, and I said "is your son around?" and she said "Queens, he's in Queens," and I said "that's not too bad," and she said "it's not nothing either," and we left it.

I want to write down that I left it. I did not follow up. I did not do the *what does your son do* thing. The conversation breathed and we let it breathe and the dryer kept turning and Beans got bored and went back behind the counter.

When the dryer buzzed I folded the stuff there, which I usually don't, I usually shove it in the bag and fold at home, but I folded it on the table because there was no reason not to. Mira said "you fold like someone whose mother taught them," and I said "she did," and Mira said "good," and I left with the bag and said "see you next week, probably," and she said "probably."

On the walk home I had this little moment of — the laundromat is, somehow, the lowest-stakes place I go. Nobody at the laundromat needs anything from me. Mira does not need me to be impressive. Mira needs me to put quarters in the correct machine and not let Beans out the front door. And inside that constraint, apparently, I am extremely capable of being a person.

I think part of why I'm writing about this instead of an Avery moment is that with Avery I'm aware of trying. I'm working on it. The trying is real and the trying is mostly good but the trying is there. With Mira I am not trying. There's nothing to try at. And it turns out that when there's nothing to try at, I am, briefly, fine. I am the bag person. The bag person is, apparently, easy to be.

Plant: four leaves. The bag is by the door. The zipper is still broken.

entries.prompt: When did you most recently feel a small flicker of envy — quick, maybe a little ugly, gone before you'd quite admitted it was there? Tell me who it was about, what they had or did, and what you found yourself thinking right after.
entries.content:
Friday around 11, Slack, the team channel. Reese posted a thing Drew had asked about — quick update, three lines, the kind of post that is half a status and half a flag-planting — and at the bottom of the post Reese had tagged a person I didn't recognize. *thanks @ for the assist.* I clicked the name. Some senior person from another org. Director-something. Profile picture in front of a window with good light.

The flicker was — fast. Like a breath. *Reese has a person.* Reese has a person two levels above us who does Reese assists. Reese has built, in six months and change of head start, a small bridge to somebody whose name I didn't even know was a name on the org chart, and the bridge is functional enough that Reese can casually @ them in a public channel and have it land as confident-but-respectful instead of weird.

I do not have a person. I have Drew, who is my person by assignment, and I have nobody else. There is no senior-anybody I could @ in a thank-you. There is no bridge. There is, where Reese has a bridge, just a gap I had not, until Friday at 11, registered as a gap.

Gone in maybe two seconds. I scrolled past, opened the doc I was supposed to be working on, started typing. Felt the flicker get stuffed down by the doing. The doing is a good muffler. I got maybe four sentences into the doc and then I noticed the flicker hadn't actually gone, it had just gotten quieter. It was running underneath the typing the way a fridge hum runs underneath a quiet room. *Reese has a person. Reese has a person.*

What I found myself thinking, right after, in order, in the actual order they came:
