One. *How did Reese even meet that person.* Like a logistics question, the way you ask logistics questions when you are trying to find a procedural reason for something so you don't have to feel the thing under it. Was it a meeting. Was it some onboarding overlap I missed because I started later. Did Drew introduce them. Did Reese just — reach out. Cold. Slack DM. *Hi, I'm Reese, I noticed you work on X, would love fifteen minutes.* That is a thing people do. I have read about people doing that. I have not done that. I have, in fact, specifically not done that, because every time the thought has surfaced I have routed it through the calculator and the calculator has come back with *too forward, will look thirsty, what if they say no, what if they say yes and you have nothing to say.*

Two. *Reese must come across older than Reese is.* This was the catty one. The flicker's small ugly cousin. *Reese must do that thing of being twenty-six but presenting as thirty-two.* As if the bridge is a trick of presentation rather than a result of just — building it.

Three. *You could do that.* Quieter, almost shy, almost the under-voice that doesn't usually get a turn. *You could DM somebody. You could ask Drew to introduce you to somebody. You could go to the thing the company puts on twice a year that you skipped both times.* The under-voice was tentative. The under-voice was, I noticed, a little embarrassed, the way it gets embarrassed when it suggests something the calculator is going to dress down.

Four. The calculator dressing it down. *On what pretext though. You'd need a reason. You'd need a project. You'd need to not look like you were reaching out for the sake of reaching out. You don't have a thing to reach out about. Reese had a thing. That's why the @ exists. Reese was working on something the senior person was already involved in, presumably, and the relationship is downstream of the work. You can't shortcut that. Don't @ anyone. Just keep doing your list.*

Five. *Keep doing your list. Keep doing your list.* The familiar phrase. The phrase that has been the answer to almost every flicker for eight months. *Keep doing your list* is what I tell myself instead of doing anything else, and the reassurance of the phrase is that it gives me permission to keep being the appliance — clean, solid, reliable — while around me other people are, apparently, building bridges.

Six, this one slower, almost a footnote. *You don't actually want Reese's person. You want the having of a person.* Same shape as the LinkedIn lurch on the bus. Same shape as the *thrilled to announce* hunger. I don't even know what Reese's person does. I clicked the profile and read the title and didn't read the bio. I did not care, particularly, who they were. I cared that Reese had one and I didn't. The wanting is structural. The wanting is *I want to be a person who has a person.* It does not specify the person.

I closed Slack at some point and went back to the doc. Worked for about forty minutes. Got something done. The flicker did not come back at full volume but it was there in the background the rest of the morning, a low hum, *Reese has a person, Reese has a person.*

What I want to write down — and I want to write it down before I lose it — is that the flicker was fast enough that I almost didn't catch it, but slow enough that the calculator caught it for me and turned it into a small loop I then carried for an hour. The flicker itself was a tiny honest thing. The loop was the calculator's translation of the flicker into something it could keep me busy with. *Reese has a person* is honest. *How did Reese meet them, you could do that, on what pretext though, keep doing your list* is the calculator pretending to think.

I don't know what to do about the bridge gap. I'm not going to DM a senior-anybody this week. I'm probably not going to DM one next week either. But I want to log that I noticed the gap exists. I had been operating, I think, on a model where the only person I needed was Drew, and where the only question was whether Drew thought I was good. Friday's flicker was the first time I clearly registered that the org has more than one node and that some of my peers have been quietly wiring themselves into more of it while I have been focused on the one node I was assigned.

That is information. I am writing it in the journal, not the notebook, because the notebook would turn it into a list of senior-people-to-DM and I would never DM any of them and the list would become another small wire in the wall. Here it can just be a thing I noticed.

Plant: four leaves, one looking maybe a little yellow at the tip. We'll see.

entries.prompt: There's a difference between being tired and being tired of holding a particular shape. When did you last want to drop the shape — at work, with someone in your family, in a conversation you didn't want to be in — and what did dropping it, even for a second, look like or almost look like?
entries.content:
Thursday, the 1:1 with Drew. The little fifteen-minute one. Same room, same chair, same small glass box.

I want to be careful here because I almost did it. I almost dropped the shape. I didn't, fully — I want to be honest about that, the dropping was more of a wobble than a drop — but the wobble was real, and I want to write down what it felt like in the half-second when the shape almost went.

Drew opened with the usual *how's it going, what are you working on,* and I had — I had the thing prepared. Three items. Transitions. The closing line. By now the shape is so practiced it loads automatically, like an autocorrect for my whole face. I started in. *Yeah, so this week I've been — *

And then Drew, halfway through my second item, did this thing where Drew leaned back in the chair and said, "you doing okay though? you look kinda beat."

You look kinda beat.

The shape — I want to describe what happened to the shape. It was like a dish I was holding with both hands and somebody bumped my elbow. The dish didn't fall. But there was a half-second where I felt where my hands were, where I felt how much I had been holding, where I felt the weight of the holding. And in that half-second the dropping was right there. It was a real option. It would have looked like — *honestly, Drew, no, I'm running on fumes, I have been lying awake about a sentence you said in standup three weeks ago, I cannot tell if I am doing okay or not and the not-knowing is the part that is wearing me down, can you just tell me, am I okay.*

That whole sentence was in my mouth. I want to write that down because I have, in this journal, a few times now, said the actual thing was in me and unsayable. This time it was sayable. It had reached my mouth. It was right there at the exit.

What I said was: "yeah, just a long week, but the work is in good shape."

But the work is in good shape.

I added the second clause myself. Drew had not asked about the work. Drew had asked about me. And I, on reflex, on the same reflex that turned *I'm fine* into *I joined a gym actually,* converted *me* back into *the work* before the answer left. *Yeah, just a long week* would have been honest. *Yeah, just a long week, but the work is in good shape* is the shape reasserting itself, putting the dish back on the table, wiping its hands on its pants.

But here is the thing. Drew did not let me put the dish back. Drew said, "okay, I hear you, but — you know if there's stuff getting in the way, you can flag it, right. Like that's a thing you can do."

That's a thing you can do.

I sat there. I had not expected the door to be opened twice in one meeting. Drew has, in eight months, opened the door this clearly maybe twice total, and I have closed it both times. And here was Drew, opening it again, and saying, basically, *I have noticed you don't open this door, this is me telling you the door exists.*

I almost said it. I almost said *can I ask you something straight out — am I doing okay here.* I had the words. I felt the shape wobble. I felt it tilt.

What I said was: "yeah, no, thanks, I appreciate that. I'll, uh — I'll keep that in mind."

I'll keep that in mind. Like Drew was a workshop facilitator. Like Drew had handed me a workbook.

Drew nodded — not the half-nod, a slightly tired full one — and said *cool* and we went back to the items, and I got through them, and the closing line landed exactly where I had pre-built it to land, and we were done in eleven minutes instead of fifteen, which is its own kind of data, I think.

I walked back to my desk and sat down and the dish was back in my hands and it felt heavier than before, because I had felt the weight of it, and you can't unfeel that.

What dropping the shape would have looked like, even for a second — I think it would have looked like silence. Just a beat of silence after the *you look kinda beat,* a beat where I didn't immediately produce the *yeah, just a long week.* The silence would have been the drop. Drew would have heard the silence and the silence would have said *I am not going to give you the smooth version right now,* and Drew would have either held the silence or said something else, and either way the conversation would have gone somewhere I have never been with Drew. I do not know what is on the other side of that silence. That is part of why I cannot, yet, give it.

What it almost looked like — I think it almost looked like me saying *honestly?* with a question mark. Just that one word. *Honestly?* Letting Drew respond *yeah, honestly,* and then having the next sentence be smaller than I am imagining. Not the nine-sentence thing. Just *I think I'm in my head about how I'm landing here and I don't know how to ask you about it without it being weird.* That sentence would have been the drop. That sentence is, I think, the achievable rougher version, the same way *I joined a gym but I haven't gone* would have been the achievable rougher version with my mom.

I did not say *honestly?* I said *I appreciate that.* The shape held.

But — and I want to write this down before the calculator does its thing where it converts the meeting into another loop — Drew opened the door twice. Drew has now told me, in plain words, that I can flag things. The door is, apparently, not a door I have to find. It is a door that has been there, being pointed at. The pointing is information. I am going to put it in the journal and not the notebook. The notebook would turn it into a draft DM. Here it can just be a thing Drew said, on a Thursday, in eleven minutes.

Plant: four leaves, the yellow tip is just the tip, the rest of the leaf is fine. Going to bed without the dish in my hands.

entries.prompt: Think about a recent moment when you were trying to fall asleep and your brain wouldn't let you. What was it replaying, rehearsing, or arguing with — and what were you telling yourself about why it mattered enough to keep you up?
entries.content:
Last night, like 1:15, on my back, the ceiling.

What it was replaying was the eleven-minute 1:1. Specifically the *I'll keep that in mind* part. Specifically the moment after, when I walked back to my desk. Specifically the look on Drew's face — which I am, I want to be clear, reconstructing, because I did not actually look at Drew's face long enough to register it, I was busy producing the closing line — but my brain has, in the absence of data, manufactured a version of Drew's face and is now studying it like it's evidence.

The face my brain produced was a small disappointed one. Not angry. Not annoyed. Just — a face that had tried something and watched it not work. The face of someone who held a door open for a person who walked into the wall next to it.

I lay there and I argued with it. *That's not what Drew's face looked like. You don't even know what Drew's face looked like. You're inventing a face to be hurt by.* And then immediately: *but the face is probably accurate, because Drew did open the door twice and you did decline twice and any reasonable person would, in some small way, be disappointed by that.* And then: *Drew is not invested enough in you personally to be disappointed. Drew is your manager. Drew opened the door because that's manager hygiene. Drew is not lying awake about your *I appreciate that.** And then, the small mean one: *no, but Drew is also not lying awake about Reese, and you are. The disparity is the disparity.*

That cycle ran maybe six times. I want to be honest about how many times. Six is conservative.

What I was rehearsing, alongside the replay, was the next 1:1. Trying to write, in my head, the version where I do the thing. *Hey Drew, last week you said I could flag stuff and I want to actually do that — * and then I would lose the sentence, because the sentence requires me to know what I'm flagging, and I don't, exactly. I am flagging a feeling. I am flagging *I do not know if I am doing okay.* Which is not a flag, that's a weather report. The calculator kept trying to convert the weather report into a flag — *flag the design sync reassignment, flag the lack of stretch projects, flag the no-bridge thing* — and each flag I drafted I then immediately rejected, because each flag would require me to admit I have been tracking things I am not supposed to be tracking. *I noticed you reassigned the design sync* is a sentence that reveals you cared about the design sync. Caring about the design sync is the part I cannot let Drew see I do.

So I was rehearsing the speech and rejecting every draft, like I was prepping for a presentation no one has scheduled and that I am, by my own rules, not allowed to give.

What I was arguing with — okay, this is the part I want to get to. I was arguing with the idea that the meeting last Thursday mattered. The calculator kept saying *it mattered, it mattered, Drew opened the door twice, the pattern is data, you have to do something with the data,* and another part of me kept saying *it was eleven minutes, Drew has these conversations with everyone, you are inflating it.* And the two of them were just going at each other, on the ceiling, in the dark.

What I was telling myself about why it mattered enough to keep me up: that Drew had given me a piece of information — *the door is here, you can use it* — and that information had a half-life, and if I did not use it within some window the information would expire. That if I went into the next 1:1 and did the smooth-three-items-closing-line thing, Drew would log that the door had been pointed at twice and not used twice, and would, quietly, stop pointing. And the not-pointing would be worse than the not-using, because the not-using is at least my choice and the not-pointing is Drew giving up.

That was the sentence keeping me up. *Drew is going to give up on you.* Said in the meanest possible voice.

I want to write down that I do not, in daylight, believe Drew is going to give up on me. Drew is, in daylight, a person doing manager hygiene with a slightly-tired direct report, and the door-pointing is part of the job, and there is no scoreboard in Drew's head where I am losing points each time I decline. The 1:15am calculator and the 7am calculator continue to run different versions of the world. The 1:15am calculator is convinced that everything is a referendum that's being silently scored. The 7am calculator can read Slack messages like a person.

But the 1:15am calculator was the one with the floor. So I lay there, and I rehearsed, and I argued, and at some point — I want to say 1:50 but it might have been later — I did the breath-matching thing with Avery again. Avery was breathing slow and even and I tried to find the rhythm and couldn't, my breath kept getting ahead, and instead of fighting it I just let my breath do whatever and listened to Avery's. Like Avery's breathing was a metronome I was not synced to but was at least in the same room with.

Why it mattered enough to keep me up. I think — and I want to put this carefully because I might not believe it tomorrow — I think it mattered because the door being open is scarier than the door being closed. When the door was closed I had a clean grievance. *Drew doesn't see me, Drew won't ask, Drew has favorites.* The grievance was load-bearing. It explained things. The door being open removes the grievance and replaces it with a task, and the task is mine, and I do not know how to do it, and not knowing how to do it at 24 with a manager who has said in plain words that I can flag things is a much harder thing to lie awake about than not being seen.

It is easier to be a person who is not given access than to be a person who is given access and does not use it. The first one is a thing happening to me. The second one is a thing I am doing to myself. The 1:15am calculator could feel the math shifting and was — panicking, basically, in the only way it knows how, which is by running the loop faster.

I fell asleep eventually. I don't remember how. I woke up at 7 to the alarm and the design sync sentence weighed almost nothing and the *you look kinda beat* moment weighed something but a manageable something, and the dish was, again, in my hands, and the day started.

Plant: four leaves. The yellow tip on the one is still just the tip. I'm going to ask Avery what to do about it tonight. Small. Out loud.

entries.prompt: When did you last say no to someone and feel good about it afterward — not guilty, not second-guessing, just clean? Walk me through the ask and what made the no easy that time.
entries.content:
Wednesday, like 4:50, the slack DM. From the guy from another team. The one whose name I still don't fully have — I have the first name, I do not have the last name, I have been calling him by the first name in my head and have so far avoided situations where I'd need the rest.

The DM said: *hey! random ask — could you jump on a quick call tomorrow at 3 to walk through your spec doc with a couple of folks on my team? we're trying to put something similar together and yours came up as a good model.*

Yours came up as a good model.

I read it twice. Felt the warm thing — small, brief, the kind that flickers and then has to negotiate with the calculator. *Yours came up.* Came up where, came up how, came up from whom. Drew, presumably, or one of the design folks Drew had forwarded it to. The doc has, apparently, traveled further than the printer compliment. The *kind of a template* sentence I told the guy from another team in the kitchen — turns out it was, retroactively, less of a stretch than I'd thought. Or the stretch had become true while I was busy feeling guilty about it. Either way.

And then the second wave. Tomorrow at 3. Tomorrow at 3 I had — let me check, I had checked, I knew — tomorrow at 3 I had blocked off because I was supposed to send a thing to Drew by EOD and the morning was already going to be eaten by standup and the design sync (which Reese now runs, which I am still on, which I am still going to, which is a thing I have decided to just keep going to without making it a story). Tomorrow at 3 was the last clean stretch I had to actually do the thing.

The calculator started up, faster than usual, with the full menu: *say yes, it's only thirty minutes, you can squeeze the thing into the morning, you'll figure it out, this is a chance to be visible to another team, this is exactly the kind of thing Reese would say yes to, the doc is the thing you've actually been praised for, you should defend its visibility, you should be the person who walks people through it, what if you say no and the guy from another team tells the people on his team that you weren't available and they go to someone else who has a similar doc — *

And then, in the middle of the calculator's run, I noticed I was doing the calculator's run. Like — I noticed it the way you notice a song you'd been singing along to without meaning to. And I just — stopped. Mid-cycle.

What stopped me, I think, was the *when do you need it by* sentence. The five-word sentence I'd written down for myself months ago after the Reese-doc Friday and never actually used. It was sitting there in the journal, in some past entry, and it just — surfaced. Not as a recipe, not as a thing I had to deploy. More like a tool I knew where it was now.

I typed back: *hey! flattered you guys are looking at it. tomorrow at 3 doesn't work — I'm heads down on something for Drew. could we do Monday? or I could send you a few annotated comments on the doc itself if that's faster.*

Sent.

I want to write down what I noticed in the sending. I noticed I did not add a smiley. I did not add an *lol* or a *no worries if not.* I did not soften the no with an apology that would have made the no take more space than the no needed. I just said the thing. *Doesn't work.* Two words. *I'm heads down on something for Drew* — true, and also a small useful flag, because it lets the guy know my time is allocated, and the allocation is to Drew, which is information that lives in his head now without me having to pretend it does. Then *could we do Monday* — actual alternative, not performative. *Or I could send annotated comments* — second alternative, actually probably better for everybody, because I write better than I talk and they could read at whatever pace.

He wrote back in like ninety seconds. *honestly comments would be amazing, no rush, whenever this week. thank you!!*

Honestly. The adverb. It didn't crack me open the way Drew's *genuinely* did, but it landed warm. And the *amazing* was clearly hyperbole, a slack hyperbole, the *amazing* you say when someone solves a small logistics problem for you. I did not let the calculator weigh it.

What made the no easy this time. Let me try to actually answer the prompt instead of orbiting it.

One: the ask was lower-stakes than I initially registered. The *yours came up as a good model* triggered the visibility hunger immediately, and the visibility hunger almost made me upgrade the ask in my head into something it wasn't. A thirty-minute walkthrough is not a referendum. It is a meeting. I would have gone in and probably overprepared and come out depleted and the guy and his team would have gotten approximately the same value they will get from my comments on the doc, which I can write in forty minutes on the bus.

Two: I had a real thing in the way. The Drew thing. Not invented, not exaggerated, not *I'm swamped* dressing up *I'm tired.* An actual deliverable with an actual deadline. The no was supported by a fact, and the fact was load-bearing in a way that meant I didn't have to do the apologetic curl I usually do when I say no.

Three — and this is the one I want to underline — I offered an alternative that was actually better. The annotated comments thing. That is a thing I can do well. That is a thing that plays to the work I already did rather than asking me to perform that work back to a room. The alternative was not a softener. It was a counter-proposal that I would have preferred even if my Thursday at 3 had been wide open.

Four: I just — didn't audit it after. That's the part I want to log. I sent the message and went back to the doc and did not, for the rest of the afternoon, replay the wording. Did not wonder if *doesn't work* was too curt. Did not draft a follow-up softening message in my head. The no left my hands and stayed left.

I told Avery about it at dinner, in passing, *somebody asked me to walk their team through the spec doc and I said no but I'd send notes,* and Avery said *good,* in the unloaded way Avery says good when something is just good, and we ate.

It is now Thursday at 9:40 and I have not opened the slack thread to reread the exchange, which is the closest I can come to a personal record. The calculator has tried twice today to relitigate the wording and I have, twice, declined the relitigation. The annotated comments are half-written in a draft. Plant has four leaves. Notebook has been closed for three days. The bag's zipper is still broken.

entries.prompt: When did you last hear yourself laugh and notice it sounded a little off — too loud, too quick, not quite where the actual feeling was? Tell me who you were with and what the conversation had been doing just before.
entries.content:
Tuesday after work, the thing for Reese. Which I want to log first because I am still figuring out how I ended up there. Reese hit a small milestone — some sign-off on the project Reese has been heads-down on for a month — and dropped a *anyone want to grab a drink, on me* in the team channel around 4. Three people said yes. Then Drew said *I can do one, briefly,* which retroactively made it not-optional in the way these things become not-optional, and I said *sure, in,* and at 5:45 we were all at the place two blocks from the office that has the long tables and the bad lighting.

I had decided on the walk over that I was going to be normal. That was the actual word I used in my head, *normal.* Not impressive, not measured, not on. Just normal. A person at a drink with coworkers. The walk was four minutes and I spent all four minutes rehearsing what normal would look like, which is, I'm aware now, not what normal people do.

We sat. Reese was at the head of the table, which felt right and also pricked. Drew was across from me. The other two — call them the two from the other pod — were on either side. Drinks came. Reese did the small toast thing, *thanks for putting up with me being a nightmare for a month,* which got the appropriate small laughs, and then we were just at a table.

The conversation was fine. Drew told a story about something that had gone wrong at a previous job, the kind of self-deprecating story managers tell when they want to remind you they are also people, and it was actually a good story, the timing was good, Drew has timing, and the table laughed. I laughed too. That laugh was fine. I want to mark that one because not every laugh in the next forty minutes was, and I want to be fair.

The one I'm writing about came maybe twenty minutes in. One of the two from the other pod — the one with the glasses — was telling a story about a meeting earlier that week where someone had unmuted to eat chips. Like, you could hear the chips. The whole meeting could hear the chips. And the person eating them did not realize, for what the storyteller claimed was forty seconds, that everyone could hear. The story was good. The story had a little arc. And at the punchline — *and then she just goes, oh my god, sorry, in this completely normal voice, like she'd just bumped into someone in a hallway* — I laughed.

I laughed loud. I laughed first. I laughed in this little burst that came out of my mouth before I had quite decided how funny the story was, and the laugh was a beat ahead of Drew's laugh and a beat ahead of Reese's, and I heard it as it was happening, and it sounded — wrong. Too quick. The kind of laugh that announces *I am here and I am part of this.* Not the kind of laugh that is just laughter at a thing.

I felt it in my face right after. The face you make when you have done a slightly performative thing in front of people whose read of you matters. I picked up my drink. I looked at the drink. I took a sip I did not need.

What had been happening just before — that's the prompt and I want to actually answer it. Just before, I had been quiet for maybe seven or eight minutes. Drew's story had ended, the table had laughed, and then there had been a small lull where the two from the other pod had been talking to each other about something I didn't have a way into, some show I haven't watched, and Reese had been on the phone briefly, and Drew had been looking at the menu. And I had been sitting there with my hands around the glass, aware that I had not contributed in a while, aware that the silence was getting load-bearing in my head even though it was probably not load-bearing at the table, and the calculator had been, very quietly, asking when I was going to say something. *Anything. A question. A small adjacent story. A laugh at the next available joke. Whatever lets you re-enter.*

So when the chips story came, the laugh was waiting. The laugh was already half-loaded before the punchline arrived. And when the punchline came I did not so much laugh as deploy a laugh I had been holding.

That's the off thing. The laugh wasn't where the feeling was. The feeling was — relief, mostly. Relief that there was a thing I could clearly respond to. Relief that the lull was ending. The chips story was funny but it wasn't laugh-out-loud-first funny, and my reaction was calibrated to a different metric than how funny the story was. It was calibrated to how badly I needed back in.

Drew glanced at me. Briefly. I am not sure Drew clocked it as a thing. Drew might have been looking at me because I was across the table and Drew was at the end of a laugh and looking around. But the glance did a thing to me. I felt the laugh exposed by it. I felt — caught. And I think the catching was self-administered, because Drew's face was just Drew's face and the person who was reading it as judgment was me.

The conversation moved. Reese asked the storyteller something. Somebody got another drink. I sipped slower. I made it about another twenty minutes and then I did the *I should head out, big morning,* which was true-ish and also was me leaving while I still had control of my edges. Outside on the sidewalk it was colder than I'd expected and I walked to the bus and on the bus I did the thing where I replayed the laugh maybe four times.

What I want to write down — it is not that the laugh was a disaster. Nobody at the table is sitting at home tonight thinking about it. The laugh was a small over-laugh at a normal-funny story and it dissolved into the rest of the evening for everyone except me. But I want to log that I noticed it. The laugh told me where I was. It told me I had been at the table managing my entry-and-exit from the conversation instead of being in it. It told me the calculator had come to drinks. The calculator does not, it turns out, take Tuesday evenings off just because the meeting is over.

I am writing this at 10:40. Avery is asleep already. I did not tell Avery about the laugh. The plant has four leaves and the yellow tip is just the tip. I am going to brush my teeth and not replay it again. The laugh happened, it sounded how it sounded, the table moved on. I can move on too, in a minute, after I close this.

entries.prompt: Outside of work — somewhere this past week when you were just moving through your day, errands or transit or at home — when did a stranger or near-stranger do something small that landed on you? Tell me what they did, and what you found yourself thinking about it after.
entries.content:
Saturday morning, the coffee place. Not the one I go to on weekdays where the baristas know me. The other one, a few blocks the other direction, that I only go to on weekends because the line on weekdays is insane. So I am, there, a stranger. Which is part of what made it land.

I was in line behind a kid. I don't mean a child kid, I mean a kid kid, like nineteen or twenty, the kind of person who has the posture of someone who got to the city six months ago and is still surprised by it. He was ordering for what turned out to be three people and he was reading the order off his phone slowly, like he was worried about getting it wrong, and he kept apologizing — *sorry, one sec, sorry, and then a — sorry, hold on* — and the barista, a woman maybe my age with the tattoo of a small bird on her forearm, was just — patient. Not performatively patient. Not the customer-service patience that has a clenched jaw inside it. Just — present. She kept her face soft. She typed each thing in as he said it. When he got confused about whether the third drink was a latte or a cortado she said, *do you want to call them?* And he said *oh — yeah, can I?* And she said, *yeah, take your sec, I'll just hold here,* and stepped back from the screen, like physically gave him the space to make the call without her hovering.

I was watching this from behind him. And I had the small impatience flicker — *come on, I have things to do* — and then I noticed I did not actually have things to do, I was going to go home and drink the coffee and read on the couch, and the impatience was just the reflex impatience, the city impatience, the calculator running its little *your time is being taken* loop on a Saturday morning when my time was, in fact, not being taken in any meaningful way.

So I just stood there. He called whoever it was. Got the answer. Came back. Told the barista. She typed it in. He paid, with a card that took two tries, and apologized again, and she said *you're good, you're totally good,* and he stepped aside to wait, visibly relieved.

Then it was me. I ordered — just a drip coffee, the easiest possible order, the order you give when you want to make up for the person ahead of you taking a long time. And the barista did this thing. She looked at me, made eye contact properly, and said, *thank you for being chill about that.*

That was the thing. *Thank you for being chill about that.*

I had not done anything. I had stood in line. I had not sighed, I had not looked at my phone in a pointed way, I had not done any of the small body-language things people do when they are letting the person ahead of them know they are being inconvenienced. I had just stood there. And she had clocked the not-doing-of-it, and she had thanked me for it.

I said something like *oh, no problem, he was doing his best,* and she smiled and rang me up and that was the end of the exchange. Maybe nine seconds.

I took the coffee and sat at the little counter by the window and what I found myself thinking about, after, was the asymmetry. She had thanked me for a thing that was nothing. The not-being-impatient was so small that I had not even registered I was doing it. It was the bus pole guy in reverse — he had done a thing for me and I had not registered it, and she had registered me doing a thing I had not done. Both of them noticing in a register I am apparently not running on most days.

And then a second thing, slower. She had thanked me because most people aren't chill about it. Most people sigh. Most people do the phone-pulling-out thing. She had thanked me because the baseline she works with all day is the impatient version, and the not-impatient version was rare enough to be worth eight words. Which means — and this is the part that sat with me on the way home — most people, in line behind that kid, would have made him feel worse. Just by standing there a certain way. Just by the shape of their waiting. That is a thing you can do to a stranger by accident. Make their bad moment slightly worse with the texture of your impatience.

I have done that. I am sure I have done that. I have stood in lines doing the city body language, the *I am being inconvenienced and I want you to know* posture, and I have made some kid with three orders feel exactly the way the calculator makes me feel when I am reading Reese's nine-page doc looking for a typo. *Watched. Measured. Found.* I am, sometimes, the person at the laundromat who is not Mira. I am the one with the impatient posture, contributing to the weather of the room.

Saturday morning I happened, by accident, by not having anywhere to be, to be the other version. And a stranger thanked me for it. And the thanking did this thing where it reframed the not-doing as a doing. *Not being impatient* became *being chill,* which is a small grammatical move but it turns an absence into a presence. She gave me credit for a thing I had not been giving myself credit for, because I had not even known I was doing it.

I have been thinking about this for two days now. Not in a recipe way. Not *be chill in lines from now on.* More like — there is a version of me that exists in the negative space of how I treat strangers, and that version is sometimes a person worth thanking, and I have no map of when she shows up, but apparently she shows up sometimes, in coffee shops, when I am not paying attention to myself enough to interfere.

Plant: four leaves. Tip still yellow. Avery said maybe overwatering, so I am letting the soil dry out this week. Going to the gym tomorrow. We'll see. The bus pole guy and the chip-laugh and the barista all in one notebook now, except not the notebook, here.

entries.prompt: Think about a recent time you were genuinely looking forward to being alone — the rest of the day cleared, no one expecting anything. Tell me what you actually did with the time, and whether it turned out to be what the wanting had been about.
entries.content:
Friday, Avery left for the airport at like 5pm — going to see family for the weekend, back Sunday night. I had been looking forward to this for, no exaggeration, eight days. Since Avery had said *I'm gonna be gone Friday to Sunday* over breakfast last week and I had felt this small private spike of — *oh. yes. okay.* I had not said anything about the spike. I had said *oh nice, tell them I say hi.* But the spike had been there.

The spike had a specific content. The content was: I am going to have the apartment to myself for fifty-some hours and I am going to do — and here's where the wanting got vague — I am going to *be alone in the way I cannot be alone when Avery is here.* Not because Avery is hard to be alone around. Avery is famously easy to be alone around. We have, many times, sat in the same room not talking for an hour. But there's a difference between being alone *with* and being alone *alone,* and I had been wanting the second one in a way I had not let myself fully look at.

The wanting, when I had let myself look at it during the week, had a kind of agenda attached. *I am going to read.* *I am going to make a real meal for myself, just me.* *I am going to do the gym thing, finally.* *I am going to put music on and clean.* *I am going to write more, longer, the kind of writing I cannot do when someone might come into the room.* The agenda was, I realize now, a way to make the wanting respectable. I was not just wanting to be alone, I was wanting to be alone *for purposes.*

So Avery left at 5. I closed the door behind them. Stood in the entryway. The apartment was — quiet in the way apartments only are when one person has just left and the other person is still recalibrating to the absence. The radiator was the loudest thing.

What I actually did with the time. Friday night: ordered food. Watched two episodes of a show Avery doesn't like. Scrolled. Went to bed at midnight. Saturday: woke up at 9, which felt late and felt good. Made the pour-over. Drank it slow at the table the way I had on the Saturday morning months ago that I keep coming back to in here. Read on the couch for about an hour — actual reading, a book I have been ten pages into for three months and which I got to thirty pages into. Took a long shower. Ate cereal for lunch. Did not go to the gym. Did not clean. Wrote nothing. Lay on the couch in the afternoon and watched the light move across the wall for I don't know how long, maybe forty minutes, maybe an hour. Got up. Walked to the laundromat to do laundry I did not strictly need to do. Mira was off — her son's husband was running the place — so the laundromat was just a laundromat. Came home. Reheated leftover food. Watched another episode. Went to bed at 11. Sunday: same shape, less of it, knowing Avery was coming back.

That's the report. The thing I want to write about is the gap between what I had been wanting and what I did.

I had been wanting *aloneness for purposes.* What I did was *aloneness without purposes.* The cleaning didn't happen. The gym didn't happen. The real meal was cereal and reheated takeout. The writing was — this, now, Sunday afternoon, the only writing I have done all weekend.

And I keep trying to figure out if that's a failure or if it's the answer.

Because here's what I noticed. The aloneness without purposes was, somehow, the thing I had actually been wanting. I had been dressing it up in agenda because the agenda made the wanting legible — to me, mostly, since nobody else was going to ask. *What did you do with your weekend* requires a list. *Lay on the couch and watched the light* is not a list. But it was the thing. The light moving across the wall on Saturday afternoon was the thing the spike had been about, eight days ago, when Avery said they'd be gone.

I want to be careful about making this into a tidy realization, because I don't trust tidy realizations and also because I'm not sure how I feel about the weekend yet. There's a part of me that is, right now, on Sunday at 4:30 with Avery's flight landing in two hours, doing the small calculator run of *you wasted the time, you had fifty hours and you read thirty pages and watched television, Avery would have made the time count, Reese would have made the time count, your mother would be appalled.* That voice is real and it is running.

But underneath it there's a quieter thing, which is — I am rested. Actually rested, in a way I have not been in months. The fan in my head, the laptop fan, the one that was loud through the whole conference room week — it has been off all weekend. I noticed it was off this morning when I made coffee. The kitchen was quiet and so was I. Nothing was running underneath. I was just a person in a kitchen.

And I think the agenda I had been mentally compiling all week — gym, clean, cook, write — was the calculator's version of how to be alone. The calculator does not know what to do with empty time. The calculator wants to convert empty time into output, because output is the only thing the calculator can read. So the calculator had pre-filled the weekend with deliverables, and I had been calling that *looking forward to being alone,* when actually what I had been looking forward to was the calculator being off, and the calculator had been trying to come along for the ride.

The calculator did come along, partially. I felt it Saturday afternoon, watching the light, when it tried to start a small loop about whether I should be doing something. The loop got maybe three sentences in and then just — stopped. Like it ran out of fuel. Because there was no audience. Avery wasn't there to perform productivity for. Drew wasn't there. Reese wasn't there. The calculator runs on witnesses and there were no witnesses, and after a while it gave up and let me watch the light.

Avery's going to walk in around 7 and ask how my weekend was. I am going to say *good, quiet, I read some,* and that is going to be true and also it's going to leave out the actual texture, which is that I lay on the couch like an animal in a sunbeam and I think it might have been the most useful thing I have done in weeks. Not because it was useful. Because it wasn't, and I let it not be, and I am, on the other side of it, less tight.

Plant: four leaves. The yellow tip is fading back to green. Avery was right about overwatering.

entries.prompt: When did you last get a piece of news — about someone else's life, a decision, a change, anything — and find that your first reaction wasn't the one you said out loud? Tell me what you heard, what you said, and what was actually moving underneath.
entries.content:
Thursday evening, group chat. Priya. Just a message, no preamble: *hey friends, quick news — I'm moving to London in March, got a thing at [org I won't write because I'd butcher it]. tell you all more soon, it's been a lot.*

A thing in March. London.

Ben replied with three exclamation points and *PRIYAAA* in caps within like twenty seconds. Nat replied a minute later with a string of emojis and *omg tell us everything when you're ready.* I was on the bus when it came in, and I sat there with the phone in my hand for I want to say a full minute before I typed anything, watching the typing-indicator ghost on Ben's side appear and disappear like he was drafting a longer message on top of the exclamation points.

What I typed was *priyaaaaa this is huge, so happy for you, want to hear all of it.* I added a heart. I sent it. It looked like the right thing on the screen.

What was moving underneath, in order, on the bus:

First — and I want to write this even though it's the worst one — *London.* Not *that's incredible.* Just the word, with a small drop attached to it. Because London is — far. London is six hours of time difference and a flight I am not going to take this year. London is the version of *the friendships are coasting* with an additional structural barrier added. I read *I'm moving to London in March* and the thing that surfaced first was not happiness, it was a small calculation about the chat. The chat is going to get harder. The chat is going to have a person in it for whom 9pm here is 2am there. The chat is going to thin further.

Second, almost on top of the first — *a thing.* *A thing at —.* Priya is not a person who calls things *a thing.* Priya, in the version of her I have in my head, is precise. *A thing* meant Priya was doing the polite thing of underselling something that was, presumably, big. Big enough to move countries for. I clicked through nothing because there was nothing to click through, it was a chat message, but I wanted to know what the thing was, in the same way I wanted to know what made Reese's senior person worth tagging, in the same way I want to know what specifically makes anyone's announcement worth the announcement. I wanted the receipts. And underneath that — Priya had not told me first. Or had not told me separately. Which is, I want to be clear, completely fine, we are not in a tier of friendship that requires advance notice, the chat is the chat. But the calculator did the small petty math anyway. *Ben might have known. Nat might have known.* Probably neither of them did, probably the chat was the announcement, but the calculator does not check, the calculator just runs the worst version.

Third — *I have not done a thing.* This was the one that made the bus feel warm. Priya, who Ben said was incredible at brunch, who does the community thing on her own time, is now also moving to London for a thing. Priya is accumulating. Priya has a stack. Priya is the kind of person about whom, in five years, someone will say *yeah, she's been doing X since college and now she's at Y in London,* and the sentence will have the clean structure of a profile. I have been at my job eight months, eight and a half now, and I have a spec doc and a printer compliment and a gym membership I have used twice. The flick was fast and ugly and I am writing it down because pretending I didn't have it would be its own kind of stretch.

Fourth, slower — *good for her.* This one surfaced after I'd typed the message. Like the genuine version arrived after the performance version had already left my hands. I sat with my phone face down on my leg for the rest of the bus ride and let the *good for her* expand. Priya has wanted out for a while, I think. Priya has, in retrospect, been talking around something for like a year — vague references in the chat to *figuring stuff out,* a voice memo Ben mentioned at brunch where Priya was venting about her city. And now there is a thing, in March, in London, and Priya is doing the thing I have not been able to picture myself doing, which is moving toward something instead of away from a fear of it. The *good for her* was real. It got to me late but it got to me.

What I said out loud, when I got home and Avery was making rice — *Priya's moving to London for some kind of fellowship thing in March, isn't that wild* — and Avery said *oh wow, good for her, are you bummed,* and I said *no, no, I'm psyched for her,* which was true and also not the whole sentence. The whole sentence was *I'm psyched for her and also a little wrecked about the friendship math and also embarrassed that my first reaction was about me and also impressed in the specific way that hurts a little.*

I didn't say the whole sentence. But I did say a piece of it, later, when we were eating. I said, *I think I'm gonna be a little sad about it too, like she's going to be really far,* and Avery said *yeah, that's allowed,* and went back to the rice. *That's allowed.* Avery does this — gives me permission for a feeling I had not realized I was asking permission for. And the feeling, once permitted, got smaller. Not gone. But smaller.

I sent Priya a separate message later, just to her. *seriously congrats, I want to hear about the thing when you're ready, also I'm gonna miss you on this side of the ocean.* She wrote back at like 1am her time with a long voice memo I haven't listened to yet. I'm going to listen to it tomorrow on the bus. Twice, probably.

Plant: four leaves, tip fully green again.

entries.prompt: Last time you wanted something for yourself and didn't quite ask for it — at home, at work, with a friend — what was the want, and what did you settle for instead?
entries.content:
Wednesday, the 1:1 with Drew. Same room, same chair, the third one in a row where I have been sitting on the *I'll keep that in mind* from the week before like it was a dish I never put down.

Drew opened with the usual. I did the usual. Three items, transitions, the closing line — and I want to write down that I noticed, this time, while I was doing it, that I was doing it. I could feel the autopilot loading, the little efficient thing, and underneath the autopilot there was a sentence trying to get out, a sentence I had actually drafted on the bus that morning, in my head, not even in the notebook. The sentence was: *can we set up a longer one — like thirty minutes, sometime this month — I want to talk about what kind of work I should be aiming at.*

That was the want. Not a flag exactly. A bigger conversation. A real one. The thirty minutes was the specific part — I had even thought about how to frame the time, because fifteen minutes is the format we have and asking for thirty would mean asking for a different format, and asking for a different format is the kind of thing the calculator told me would *signal something.* What it would signal I could not have said precisely. *Need.* That you are a person who needs.

I had the sentence on the bus. I had it in the elevator. I had it walking down the hallway. I had it as I sat down. Drew said *what are you working on,* and the autopilot took over, and the sentence stayed where it was, and the closing line landed, and Drew did the half-nod, and we had four minutes left.

Drew said, "anything else?"

Anything else. The third opening. I want to log that this is the third one. Drew has now, in three consecutive 1:1s, given me a clean opening, and I am keeping the count, which means at some level I know what's happening, and I am still — still — not using them.

What I said was: "actually, yeah, I had a small thing — could I get your take on the design folks' feedback on the doc? like just whether you think the directions they pushed in are the right ones to keep going."

A small thing. A small thing. I downsized the want in my mouth before it had finished forming. The thirty-minute conversation became *your take on a piece of feedback.* The *what kind of work should I be aiming at* became *which of these directions to keep going.* I took the actual question — the big one, the one that had been keeping me up — and I shaved it into a doc-shaped question, because doc-shaped questions are inside the format Drew and I already have, and the format is the thing I cannot, apparently, ask to expand.

Drew gave a thoughtful answer. Drew is good at thoughtful answers when the question is doc-shaped. Drew talked for maybe three minutes, said useful things, recommended I push harder on one of the directions, said the other one was probably going to die naturally, told me to come back if I got stuck. I nodded. I wrote things in the notebook — yes, the notebook, I had it open, I had reverted on the not-replacing thing, the elastic is fine for now. Drew looked at the clock. We were a minute over. We wrapped.

I walked back to my desk and the gap between what I had asked and what I had wanted to ask was so visible to me it felt like a physical thing in the hallway. *Which direction to keep going* is a question whose answer affects, like, two weeks of work. *What kind of work should I be aiming at* is a question whose answer affects the next two years. I had, in the same eleven minutes, traded the second one for the first one, on reflex, and Drew had — fairly, kindly — answered the question I had asked, and was now back at his desk with no idea I had been holding a different question.

What I settled for. The doc-direction answer is genuinely useful. I want to be honest about that. I will use it. The next two weeks of work will be better for it. So the settling produced something. The settling was not nothing. But the settling is also — it is doing the thing I keep doing, which is taking a thing I want and breaking off the smallest sayable piece of it and presenting that piece as the whole order. *I joined a gym, actually.* *Kind of a template.* *Just a long week.* And now: *could I get your take on the feedback.* All of them are versions of the same move. Take the want, find the doc-shaped piece, hand over the doc-shaped piece, walk away with the rest still in my chest.

The rest is still in my chest. I can feel it sitting there at 9:50. The thirty-minute conversation has not happened and I do not know when it will happen and the most likely answer is *next 1:1, when Drew opens the door for the fourth time, you will downsize again.* Because the format is the format, and asking for a different format is the want underneath the want, and the want underneath the want is harder to ask for than the want.

I am not going to make a recipe out of this tonight. I am not going to write *next time I will ask for thirty minutes.* I have written that sentence in this journal, in different forms, a number of times, and the writing of it is part of how I let myself off the hook for not doing it. *I have written it down, so I have engaged with it, so I have done the work.* I am declining the trick this time.

I will say what I noticed. I noticed I had a real want. I noticed I had a usable sentence ready. I noticed I had an opening Drew handed me. I noticed I shaved the want down to a doc question and used the opening for the doc question. I noticed all of it in the moment, which is new, and I did the smaller version anyway, which is not new. The noticing is, apparently, ahead of the doing. Maybe by a lot. Maybe the noticing has to walk the same hallway five or six more times before the doing catches up.

Plant: four leaves, all green. Going to bed.

entries.prompt: Tell me about something you bought recently — anything, big or small — where the wanting and the buying didn't quite line up. What did you get, and what was the gap between what you'd hoped it would do and what it actually did?
entries.content:
The headphones. The new ones. Not the old pair, which were fine, which still worked, which had a slightly fraying cable on one side but were, by any honest accounting, headphones that did the thing headphones do. The new ones I bought two Saturdays ago after about three weeks of looking at them in a tab. Over-ear, the kind with the noise-cancelling that's supposed to be the good noise-cancelling, the kind people on tech forums refer to by the model number instead of the brand because that is how serious they are about them. Two hundred and eighty dollars. I had been telling myself for three weeks I was *researching.* What I was doing was wanting them.

The wanting had a story attached. The story was: I will put these on in the morning on the bus and the bus will go quiet. I will put these on at my desk during the times when the office is loud and I cannot focus and the focus will arrive. I will put these on on the walk home and the walk home will be a thing I am inside of, instead of a thing I am crossing to get to the apartment. The story had, underneath it, a quieter story, which was: the noise I am trying to cancel is not entirely external. Some of the noise is the calculator. Some of the noise is the *kind of similar to what Reese said, basically.* And the sales copy on the website did not say *cancels the calculator,* but it kind of said it, in the way sales copy will gesture at a thing if you read it the way I was reading it.

I bought them on the Saturday after Avery's weekend away. Walked to the store. Tried them on the display unit. The noise of the store fell away in this dramatic way and I had this small lurch of *yes, this, this is it,* and I bought them right there without doing the additional comparison shopping I had told myself I was going to do. Walked home with the box. The box was nicer than the box for headphones needs to be. I noticed I liked the box. The box was part of what I was paying for and I was, in the moment, fine with that.

Sunday I unboxed them. Charged them. Put them on. Played the song I had been mentally cuing up for this moment, which, embarrassingly, was a song I have been listening to since college. Sat on the couch. The bass was — fine. The noise-cancelling was — present. The apartment got quieter, in the parts of the apartment that have noise to cancel, which on a Sunday afternoon is mainly the radiator. I sat there with the headphones on listening to the song and I waited for the thing.

The thing did not come.

I want to be specific about what I mean by the thing. The thing was — I had pre-loaded an emotional event. I had been imagining, for three weeks, how it would feel to have these. The feeling was supposed to be a kind of arrival. *Now I am the kind of person who* — and the sentence trailed off, because what I was actually buying was a sentence I couldn't finish. Now I am the kind of person who has the good headphones. Now I am the kind of person who takes their commute seriously. Now I am the kind of person who curates their auditory environment. The sentence had no real ending because the ending was just *who is okay,* and I was paying $280 to not have to say that ending out loud.

I sat on the couch with the song playing and I was the same person I had been twenty minutes earlier without the headphones. The calculator was still running. The song was just a song. The bass was just bass. The radiator was quieter. The radiator being quieter was, it turns out, the actual product. The actual product was a small reduction in ambient noise. The actual product was not what I had been buying.

I have used them every day since. I want to be fair to them. The bus is, in fact, quieter. The walk home does have a different texture. There is a real, measurable, technical thing the headphones do, and they do it well, and the $280 was not, by any consumer review standard, wasted. If you asked me *would you buy them again* I would say yes. I would say it a little defensively but I would say it.

But the gap. The gap was — I had wanted them to do a thing they cannot do. I had wanted them to lower the volume on the part of me that runs all day. And the part of me that runs all day is, it turns out, on the inside of the ear cups. The headphones cancel the bus. The headphones cancel the office HVAC. The headphones do not cancel the *kind of similar to what Reese said, basically.* That sentence is in there with me, inside the noise-cancelled bubble, and is, if anything, slightly louder when the bus has gone quiet, because there's less around it.

I noticed this on Tuesday. Tuesday morning, bus, headphones on, song playing, the sentence about the laugh at the drinks thing was running underneath the song the same way it would have without the headphones. Maybe more. Maybe the headphones gave it a little reverb. I almost laughed on the bus. Not a real laugh. The kind where you exhale through your nose because you've caught yourself.

I am wearing them right now to write this, actually. Avery is watching something in the other room and the headphones make the room I am in mine. That part is real. That part is worth something. But what I had hoped they would do — be a small purchasable quiet, a quiet I could put on like a hat — they cannot do, because the loud part is not in the room.

The Saturday morning quiet, the laundromat with Mira, the laugh on the couch, the brunch eggs. Those were free. Those were also unscheduled, and unbuyable, and have never once arrived because I summoned them. The headphones were a try at making the unbuyable thing buyable. The headphones are good headphones. The thing they were standing in for is still where I left it.

Plant: four leaves. Headphones charging on the table. Going to bed without them.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel a small wave of relief when a plan involving someone else got rearranged or shortened — a friend cancelling, a call running short, someone leaving earlier than expected? Tell me who it was, what changed, and what the relief was actually about.
entries.content:
Sunday afternoon, my parents were doing the video call. Both of them, both faces in the frame, my mom angled too close like she always is, my dad off to the side because he never quite trusts the camera to include him. We had said an hour. My mom had said *let's do an hour, we have time today,* on Friday when she'd scheduled it, and I had said *yeah, an hour sounds great,* and on Sunday at 2pm I had set up at the table with a coffee and a little internal stopwatch already running.

We got forty minutes in and my dad's phone rang. His actual phone, the landline, which still exists in their house and which still rings the way landlines ring. He looked at my mom and my mom looked at the kitchen and said *that's probably Carl about the thing,* and my dad said *honey I've got to take this,* and my mom said *we should probably wrap up anyway, your dad's been wanting to get to the hardware store,* and I said *no totally, go go,* and we did the goodbyes — *love you, love you too, talk Sunday,* the standard exit — and the call ended at minute forty-three.

Twenty minutes back. Twenty minutes I had not planned to have.

The relief was — immediate, and physical, the way it was the Nat night, the way it was the Ben night. *Oh.* My shoulders dropped about an inch. I noticed them drop. I had been holding them up the whole call without registering it.

What had I been holding. Let me try to actually answer this instead of orbit it.

The call had been fine. The call had been the regular call. My mom told me about the yoga thing, which is now going well, which is now apparently a thing she likes, which is a small reversal from the last update. My dad gave the car update — the car is fixed, again, for now — and my mom gave me the neighbor's-tree update and the recipe update and asked about Avery and asked about work, and I gave the polished versions of all of it, the *good, busy, in a good way,* the *Avery's good, we're good,* and at one point my mom said *and how's the gym, honey,* with this little hopeful inflection, and I said *you know, I've been a couple times, trying to make it more regular,* which was true in the technical sense — I have been twice — and which was the polished version of *I have been twice in seven weeks of paying for it.* I felt the small wire go into the wall as I said it.

So what I had been holding, for forty-three minutes, was the polished version. The whole stack of polished versions. Each polished version is small and sayable on its own but the maintenance of the whole stack, in real time, on video, with my parents' specific faces watching mine — that takes a kind of attention I did not realize I was paying until Carl called and I got to stop paying it.

The relief was about the stopping. Not about my parents. I love my parents. I had, in fact, been enjoying the call in the parts where the polishing was lighter — the yoga thing, my mom telling a story about a class where she fell over, my dad in the background going *she really did fall over,* my mom laughing, me laughing. That part was good. That part was not what I was relieved to be done with.

What I was relieved to be done with was the part where my mom, at minute thirty-five, had said *you sound a little tired honey, are you sleeping,* and I had said *yeah, I'm sleeping, just been a busy stretch,* and the small calculator inside the call had spun up and started running. *She is going to ask again. She is going to ask in a slightly different way. She is going to ask if I am eating. She is going to ask if Avery and I are okay. Each of those questions has a polished answer ready and each of the polished answers will require maintaining the previous polished answers without contradiction.* The call had become, around minute thirty-five, a small consistency exercise. And the consistency exercise was what I was holding up. And Carl, bless Carl, called.

I sat at the table after the call ended for a minute. Did the thing where I just looked at the closed laptop. The coffee was cold. I had not drunk much of it because I had been talking. I dumped it and made a fresh cup and stood at the counter while the kettle did its thing and I noticed, in the standing, that the apartment was very quiet, and that my chest was — looser. Not loose. Looser.

What the relief was actually about — I want to write this down because I think I have been reading my own relief wrong. I keep telling myself, in entries about Nat and Ben, that the relief is about preservation, about keeping the friendship the size it used to be, about not finding out. And maybe with Nat and Ben that's part of it. But with my parents the relief was simpler than that. The relief was about being able to stop performing okayness at people who love me and who, if I performed it any less convincingly, would worry. The performance is a kindness, in its way. The performance protects them from a version of me that would, if I let it out, send my mom into a week of follow-up calls. The performance is also exhausting, and the exhaustion is invisible to them by design, and Carl calling let me put it down twenty minutes early without anyone noticing I had been carrying it.

I want to add — and this is the part I think is new — that the wanting to put it down is information. It is information about what I am keeping from them, and about why. I am not going to call my mom back tonight and tell her about the gym, or about the design sync, or about the 1:15 ceiling. That is not the move. The move is just to log that the relief had a shape, and the shape was *I am tired of pretending the polished version is the whole report,* and the people I am most tired of pretending to are the people who would be the most upset to find out I was pretending.

Avery got home from a run around 4. Asked how the call was. I said *good, short, dad had to take a call from Carl,* and Avery said *who's Carl,* and I said *the friend with the cars,* and Avery said *ah, the cars guy,* and went to shower. Plant: four leaves. The fresh coffee is good.

entries.prompt: When you walked into your apartment yesterday and closed the door behind you, what was the first thing you did, and what was the quality of the quiet you stepped into?
entries.content:
Yesterday was Tuesday, the day with the standup and the design sync back to back and then the long thing in the afternoon with the people from another team where I sat in a chair that hurt and tried to look engaged for ninety minutes about something I had limited stake in. I left at 5:50. The bus was the bus. I had the headphones on. I got off at my stop and walked the four blocks and the sky was doing the early-dark thing that has been starting earlier every week and I had the small flash, walking, of *I do not want to talk to anyone for at least an hour.* Not in a bad way. Just as a fact about my body.

I put the key in the door and turned it and pushed it open and the first thing I did, before I even put the bag down, was take the headphones off. That was the first thing. I had been wearing them since the bus and I took them off in the entryway and held them in my hand for a second, and what I noticed was that the apartment was quieter than the headphones had been. The headphones had been playing nothing — I had turned the music off blocks ago and just kept them on as a kind of helmet — and the noise-cancelling had been doing its low electronic thing, that faint pressure-feeling on the eardrums that you stop registering until it stops. The apartment didn't have that. The apartment was just — quiet. Plain quiet. No active cancelling, no engineered hush. Just the radiator clicking and the fridge.

And I stood there in the entryway for I want to say twenty seconds with the headphones in my hand and I had this moment of — *oh, the thing I've been paying $280 to approximate is just here, for free, when nobody else is home.* Avery wasn't home. Avery had texted at 4 saying *running late, dinner with the work people,* and I had felt the small pre-relief of that, the kind I am learning to read now, and now here was the actual relief, the apartment-shaped one.

I put the bag down. I put the headphones on the little table by the door where keys go. I took my shoes off. I did not turn on a light, even though it was dim, because turning on a light would have been a small announcement to the apartment that someone was home and asking something of it, and I did not want to ask anything of the apartment yet. I walked to the kitchen in the dim and got a glass of water and drank the whole glass standing at the sink looking at nothing.

The quality of the quiet. Let me try.

It was not the Saturday morning quiet. The Saturday morning quiet, the coffee one, has a kind of light inside it — sun coming in low, the day being unstarted, possibility as a flavor. This was a different quiet. This was end-of-day quiet. The day had already happened. The quiet was on the other side of it. Nothing was about to start. I had nothing to do for the next two hours that anyone would check on. The quiet had a *finished* quality, even though the day was technically still going.

It was also not the laundromat quiet. The laundromat quiet has Mira in it, and Beans, and the radio. It has a low hum of being-with-other-people-without-needing-anything-from-them. This was the alone version of that. There was nobody to not need anything from. There was just me and the rooms.

What I want to try to say — and I am going to fumble it — is that the quiet had a kind of friendliness. That sounds wrong. Quiets do not have friendliness, quiets are absences, you cannot ascribe a tone to an absence. But the apartment, in the dim, with my bag down and the headphones on the table, felt like it was on my side. The radiator clicking sounded companionable instead of annoying. The fridge hum was a thing that lived here with me. The little smell — Avery's coffee from this morning, faint, the windowsill plant smell, the apartment's specific apartment-smell that you only notice when you've been gone all day — was familiar in a way that felt like being known. The apartment knew me. The apartment was not going to ask me anything or test me on anything or open a door I would then decline to walk through. The apartment was just going to be where I was, while I was being there.

I stood in the kitchen with the empty glass for another minute. I noticed I was not reaching for my phone. The reach-for-the-phone reflex is so automatic that the noticing of its absence was, itself, a small event. I was just standing in the kitchen looking at the empty glass like it was something. Then I refilled it. Then I walked into the living room and sat on the couch — not lay down, just sat, with my coat still on, the coat half-unbuttoned but on — and I sat there for a long time. I am going to guess fifteen minutes but it might have been more. The dim got dimmer. I did not turn on a light. The radiator clicked. At one point a car went by outside and the headlights moved across the wall and then the wall went back to being a wall.

What I kept thinking, when I thought anything, was — *nobody knows I'm here.* Avery thought I was probably home but did not know for sure. Drew did not know what I was doing. Reese did not know. My mom did not know. The chat did not know. There was a small unobserved hour. The calculator, who runs on observation, did not have a job. It sat down with me on the couch and was quiet too.

Eventually I got up. I took the coat off. I turned on one lamp, the small one, because the dim had become actual dark. I made eggs because that's what I had. I ate them at the table standing up because sitting down felt like committing to a configuration I didn't want to commit to. I wrote nothing. I read nothing. I did not go to the gym. Avery came home around 9 and the apartment recalibrated to two-people-quiet, which is also good but is a different quiet, and the entryway quiet was already gone by then, was already a thing that had happened earlier and that I was carrying, and that I am writing down now so I can find it again when I need to.

Plant: four leaves. The lamp is on. I am going to turn it off in a minute.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel like you were performing competence — at work, with friends, on a call — and underneath you weren't sure you actually had the thing you were projecting? Walk me through the moment and what you were doing to keep the surface intact.
entries.content:
Friday morning, the meeting with the design folks. The one where I was, on paper, presenting the next pass of the spec doc — the same doc, the doc that started this whole arc, the doc Drew called genuinely clean by the printer two months ago. The doc that, through some accident of forwarding, has become a thing other people look at. I had spent Thursday night thinking I would feel ready and instead had spent it adding small final passes, each one smaller than the last, the kind of editing where you change a word back to the word it was three drafts ago.

There were six people on the call. Two from design I knew, two I didn't, plus Drew, plus a person from the design lead's side who I had never met and whose title was bigger than anyone else's on the call. Director-something. The kind of title that, when I saw it pop up in the participants list, made me sit up straighter at my desk like she could see me through the camera, which, well — she could.

I was first up. I shared the screen. I said the thing I had practiced — *thanks for making the time, I'm going to walk through the updates and then I'd love to open it up* — and I started walking through.

Here's where the gap starts. I had practiced the walkthrough. I had not practiced for questions about the parts I was less sure about. There is a section in the middle of the doc — the part about how the handoff actually happens between our team and theirs, the operational layer — that I had written by basically inferring from how I had seen it work twice. I had not, ever, run that part by anyone. I had written it with the confident voice you use when you do not want anyone to notice you are guessing. The whole rest of the doc is solid. That section is a fence I painted to look like a wall.

Three minutes in, the director-something lady — I'll call her by the title because that's how I was experiencing her — unmuted and said, "sorry to jump in, can you say more about how you're imagining the handoff actually working day-to-day? like what's the trigger for our side knowing it's our turn."

What's the trigger.

I did not have a trigger. I had a sentence in the doc that said *the trigger is X,* where X was a thing I had written down because I needed something to put in that sentence. I had no information about whether X was right. I had, in fact, a small private suspicion that X was probably wrong, or at least incomplete, because the way I had described it required a system to send a signal that I was not sure the system actually sent.

What I said was, "yeah, great question — so the way we've been thinking about it is X, with the assumption that Y is in place, but I think it's worth pressure-testing whether that assumption holds on your side, because there might be a cleaner trigger we're missing."

I want to itemize what I just did there.

*Great question* — buying time. *The way we've been thinking about it* — pluralizing myself, because *we* sounds like a team has decided this and *I* sounds like I made it up. *With the assumption that Y is in place* — adding a hedge that, if Y turns out not to be in place, lets me say I had flagged it. *Pressure-testing whether that assumption holds on your side* — moving the burden of verification to her side, which sounded collaborative and was actually me pushing the question back across the table because I could not field it. *There might be a cleaner trigger we're missing* — pre-conceding ground I was not standing on anyway, dressed up as humility.

The whole sentence was a piece of architecture. None of it was a lie. All of it was scaffolding around the fact that I did not know.

She nodded. She said, "okay, that makes sense, why don't we take an action item to map the actual flow on our side and bring it back." She typed something. Drew unmuted and said, "yeah, that's a good approach, let's get that doc'd up for next week." Drew used my own *doc'd up* shape. The conversation moved.

I kept the surface intact for the rest of the call. I did the active-listening face. I made a note when the director-something-lady mentioned something about her team's review cycle that I will need to actually understand later. I asked one question I genuinely did want the answer to, which I think landed well, because she gave a real answer rather than a polite one.

We wrapped at the hour. I closed the laptop and the apartment was quiet — I had taken it from home because the design folks are on a different coast and 8am here is reasonable for them — and I sat at the table with the closed laptop and felt my heart slowly come down off whatever shelf it had climbed onto.

What I was doing to keep the surface intact: scaffolding. That's the actual word. I was building small grammatical structures around the empty places so the empty places would read as deliberate. Hedges where I would have wanted certainty. Plurals where I would have wanted to say *I.* Pre-conceded ground so nobody would have to take it from me. The vocabulary of someone-who-knows, deployed in front of the parts I do not know yet.

The competence I was projecting was *I have thought carefully about this whole thing.* The thing underneath was *I have thought carefully about most of it and improvised the middle section and you just touched the improvised part.* And the gap is — I think this is the part I want to write down — the gap is not actually huge. I do know most of it. I did do the work. The middle section is a real weakness but it's not the whole doc. The fence I painted to look like a wall is one fence in a lot of actual wall.

But standing there in the call, when she asked the question, the calculator did not do that math. The calculator went straight to *you are about to be revealed,* and the scaffolding loaded automatically. I did not choose the architecture. The architecture is, at this point, a reflex.

I'm going to email the director-something-lady this afternoon and say, plainly, *I want to be honest that the trigger section was where I had the least information, would love your team's input on what's actually realistic.* Not a recipe. Just this once. Because the doc's going around with the painted fence in it, and somebody is going to lean on it eventually, and I would rather they not.

Plant: four leaves. Coffee's gone cold while I wrote this.
